To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progressing the establishment of a regional endometriosis centre in the North West, including a (i) timeline; and (ii) breakdown of cost.
answer_text: My Department is working with the Provider Collaborative to invest across all five Trusts to enhance capacity for diagnostics, additional laparoscopy sessions and clinical appointments. Work is also underway to establish two regional Endometriosis Centres within the Western and Belfast Trusts. These centres will deliver complex surgical care for women who require it, supported by multidisciplinary teams including colorectal and urological surgeons. The development of these centres is consistent with the nationally agreed pathway for endometriosis care.The development of endometriosis services remains one of my key priorities for improving women’s health. My officials expect the submission of the relevant business cases in the coming weeks. However, current funding is insufficient to secure all of the capacity required under the agreed pathway, and therefore the expansion of the service will need to proceed on a phased basis as additional funding becomes available.In 2025/26, £3.5m was allocated for gynaecology service development. This included the expansion of Clinical ETriage across all Trusts, the introduction of Direct to Test services with increased sonography capacity, enhanced procedural nurse capacity, and the development of Phase 1 of a Northern Ireland Endometriosis Service.  Phase 1 of the Endometriosis Service maps the patient’s journey across primary care, enhanced primary care, and local, regional and tertiary services. The model is based on the pathway designed by the Endometriosis Task and Finish Group and ensures that patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right practitioner. As part of this pathway, Regional Centres in the Belfast and Western Trusts will be developed and expanded to manage women with severe disease. These multidisciplinary, accredited services will provide the level of care and support that endometriosis specialists have long advocated for in Northern Ireland. This model enables women to access tertiarylevel expertise while ensuring that followup care is delivered as close to home as possible.The outcome of bids for additional gynaecology investment from the £80m Elective Care funding, expected in April 2026, is still awaited.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has given any consideration to amending the minimum standards for nursing care, residential care homes and domiciliary carers to ensure social care providers and direct care staff working in older adults’ care including people living with dementia in other settings, undertake best practice dementia training.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of plans to upgrade the Caw Roundabout, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of Caw Roundabout is a transport mitigation measure arising out of the adjacent H30 Rosses Gate residential development. As a developer led scheme, Southbank Square is responsible for design and construction of the works. My Department’s role is to ensure the design meets the required standards and the final built road layout is safe for all road users.My officials continue to work with the developer to progress the detailed design of the Caw Roundabout upgrade. An updated design package was submitted by the developer on 3rd April and is currently under consideration.I have committed additional resource to ensure a timely review of all elements of the detailed design.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the recent rising cost of fuel, whether his Department has given any consideration to reviewing current private sector school transport contracts.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including the management of contracts it holds with private transport operators. The EA has advised me that it acknowledges the concerns of private operators and it continues to monitor developments in fuel costs, however at present, it has no plans to uplift the contracted rates agreed for delivering home to school transport.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rational as to why his Department removed ring fencing from youth services.
answer_text: Historically, the budget for youth services has been a standalone budget within the overall Education budget, allocated by my Department to the Education Authority (EA) specifically to meet the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities.Going forward, a single composite Block Grant allocation will be provided to the EA for both Schools and Youth services.  The rationale for this change is to bring Youth funding into line with other established education budgets and to give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality.I would emphasise that all spending remains subject to legislation, policy, and Departmental oversight.  In short, the statutory requirement to secure adequate provision of youth activities remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department has given to mitigating the impact of changes to the Local Growth Fund.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any assessment that has been made of the challenges currently faced by homeless service providers in relation to the recruitment and retention of staff.
answer_text: Firstly, I would like to once again place on record my thanks to homeless service providers for the vital work that they do, often in very difficult circumstances. I want to protect funding to the Housing Executive to deliver homelessness services, and despite the lack of an agreed budget, in my interim allocation to the Housing Executive I have once again ringfenced funding for this.Homeless service providers deliver vital support for vulnerable people, and they deserve to be properly and consistently funded.Upon coming into office, I moved quickly to address sustained funding pressures within the Supporting People programme, whose budget had remained static over a number of years. I provided an immediate additional £3 million, followed by a further £4.8 million in 2024/25, to support the continued viability of these essential services.  In 2025/26, I allocated an additional £1m for pipeline schemes to fund new and extended Supporting People services, followed by a further £1.98m of in-year funding to address staffing and inflationary costs.These increases have raised the Supporting People budget from £72.8 million to £83.7 million.In addition to the £4.3m for community prevention activities, in 2025/26, I ringfenced an additional £2.5m for strategic homelessness prevention, and provided an initial £3m for the New Foundations pilot.  Those ringfences will continue this year.While neither my Department, nor the Housing Executive, are the direct employer for Supporting People staff and therefore, from a contractual perspective, have no role in workforce recruitment and retention, I have done everything possible to support those who are.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any planned steps his Department is taking in response to the 19 Recommendations set out in the February 2026 ADHD Needs Assessment Report.
answer_text: The final ADHD Needs Assessment Report was published on 12 February 2026, noting that any decisions on future commissioning of ADHD services would be in the context of overall affordability.Officials have commenced an options appraisal process to identify what options could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to help address the need for ADHD services.  The outcome of this work will inform the development of an implementation plan to address the recommendations in the Needs Assessment report, which is already underway.  It is recognised that several of the recommendations can only be progressed in the context of a future commissioned service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any work being undertaken by her Department to improve the Needs Assessment Service, within the Victim and Witness Care Unit.
answer_text: As part of the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, my Department undertook scoping and analysis of how fee capping models operate in practice, including the funding, regulatory and delivery mechanisms required to support them. There would be significant cost, legislative and operational implications associated with such an approach. For these reasons, the capping of fees is not currently proposed within the draft Strategy but will be kept under consideration. The public consultation on the draft Strategy closes on 24 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's review of the Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services.
answer_text: As a general principle, patients are expected to make their own way to healthcare facilities in non-emergencies, either by using their own transport, that of friends or relatives, or by using schemes that exist to help people who have transport difficulties. While the existing Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland remains broadly in line with current national guidance, it was last updated in 2007.  Given the passage of time, I have asked my officials to undertake a review and ensure it provides an equitable, fit for purpose HSC Transport Strategy that is available across the region and meets the needs of service users and carers in all Trust areas.Current provision of non-emergency transport is complex with a variety of approaches in place across Trusts.  In partnership with the Trusts, my officials have established an HSC Transport Strategy Review Group to understand those complexities and to undertake an evidence led review.  Work to gather relevant Trust data is ongoing, as are discussions on the best way forward to ensure the review will yield the most effective patient focused outcome, within available resources.  Officials from my Department also sit on the Cross Departmental Community Transport Working Group led by the Department for Infrastructure and I will continue to seek appropriate opportunities for collaborative cross departmental working to reduce costs and improve service quality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has taken to support the Western Health and Social Care Trust to install fully compliant Changing Places facilities in acute and community hospitals.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has taken to ensure that all Health and Social Care Trusts hold a register of Changing Places facilities at acute and community hospitals.
answer_text: No actions have been taken by my department to ensure that all Health and Social Care Trusts hold a register of changing places facilities at Acute and Community Hospitals. No specific actions have been taken by my department to support the Western Health and Social Care Trust to install changing places facilities. The assessment of need for these facilities in existing premises is an operational matter for individual Trusts to undertake.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to commission a community-based audiology service for adults, to improve access to assessment and treatment.
answer_text: As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced and is restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term.Officials are currently scoping the potential of a pilot proposal for an alternative communitybased audiology model to complement existing HSC services. This potential approach would allow a small group of patients to be seen in a new way in the short term, with the intention of gathering evidence to inform a longerterm solution.  The outcome of the scoping exercise will inform the next steps and any associated funding implications of service extension.

answer_text: As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced and is restricting capacity. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement underway between her Department and the Department of Health to address the continued lack of data collection on the number of suicides following domestic abuse, similar to the approach taken in England and Wales.
answer_text: I recognise the profound concern around suicides which may follow experiences of domestic abuse. This is an extremely complex and sensitive area which goes well beyond the remit of any single department, including the Department of Justice.  As such, any proposal to identify and record suicides where domestic abuse may have been a contributory factor would require careful consideration across a number of agencies and departments.The Department of Health does not have responsibility for the recording of deaths in Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) which falls under the remit of the Department of Finance are responsible for the collation of death statistics. The outcome of the coronial investigation, including the formulation of the cause of death, the recorded study category and the NISRA coding process reflects the statutory focus on how the deceased died, rather than why they died. Any decisions around enhancing data collection, for example through academic research, would require consideration by a range of stakeholders.Department of Health has also confirmed that the Lifeline service does collect data on main presenting issues and could identify the number of calls where domestic violence has been a factor; however, suicide deaths data would need to be collected separately through NISRA and other relevant stakeholders who may hold that information.Officials in my Department engage regularly with the Department of Health through established cross-departmental structures under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031. That Strategy, led jointly by both departments, recognises the serious and sometimes fatal consequences of domestic abuse and includes workstreams focused on improving data, evidence and learning. Through these structures, officials continue to consider how data collection can be strengthened and how learning from other jurisdictions may inform future development in Northern Ireland.While this is not a straightforward matter of data capture alone, I am clear that improving our understanding of the links between domestic abuse and suicide is essential. My Department will continue to work closely with the Department of Health and other relevant partners to explore what further steps may be possible within the existing statutory framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any policy scoping exercises currently being undertaken by her Department in relation to extending the existing Domestic Homicide Review model to include domestic abuse-related deaths by suicide.
answer_text: I remain committed to exploring how the Domestic Homicide Review (DHR) model might be widened in future to include domestic abuserelated deaths by suicide and I recognise the additional learning that this could lead to.However before any formal scoping exercise can commence, including assessing the operational readiness of all agencies and organisations involved in DHRs, it is essential that the current system is operating efficiently and sustainably. My priorities to date have been to embed the existing DHR process, progress reviews, respond to the recommendations arising from those reviews and ensure that learning is shared effectively across agencies and wider society. Our very early assessment, based on the experience in England and Wales has indicated that significant additional investment from the Executive and relevant agencies would be required before we can consider extending the model to include domestic abuse related deaths by suicide. This does not in any way diminish the importance of domestic abuserelated suicides, and the families and communities who have been left devastated by these deaths remain firmly in my thoughts. Rather, it reflects the additional complexity of such cases and the need to ensure that any future expansion of the DHR model is robust, properly resourced and capable of delivering meaningful learning. My officials continue to maintain regular communication with colleagues across other UK jurisdictions, monitoring their procedures, practice and emerging learning. My Department remains committed to continual improvement of the current model and to strengthening the positive impact of DHRs wherever possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 38267/22-27, whether a timeframe is available for when responses to her consultation on proposals to criminalise sexually explicit deepfake images will be publicly available.
answer_text: The responses to the public consultation on proposals to criminalise the creation and sharing of sexually explicit deepfake images of adults have been analysed and a summary document, which will also provide my decision on the way forward, will be made available as early as possible in advance of Consideration Stage of the Justice Bill.  Given the tight timescales involved, my officials have prioritised finalising drafting instructions for the provisions. Whilst my Department has a number of competing priorities, this is one of the highest for me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding that has been ring-fenced in 2026 and beyond, to deliver the bowel cancer screening commitments in the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-32.
answer_text: Budgets have not yet been agreed for future years and at this point no funding has been ring-fenced in 2026 and beyond to deliver the bowel cancer screening commitments in the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland (2022-32).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any timeline his officials are working to for (i) approval of the Public Health Agency's business case and implementation plan to extend bowel cancer screening to those from the age of 50; and (ii) commencement of phased implementation.
answer_text: A business case to extend bowel cancer screening has been submitted to the Department by the Public Health Agency and is currently being considered by officials.As my Department’s final Budget settlement for 2026/27 onwards has not  been agreed by the Executive, I am not currently in a position to provide any update on potential next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has identified the additional diagnostic capacity required to support bowel cancer screening age expansion to those aged 50 to 74.
answer_text: A business case, including the additional diagnostic capacity required, has been submitted to my Department by the Public Health Agency and is currently being considered by officials.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking (i) to identify any potential future reduction of medicine availability; and (ii) to secure future medicine supplies.
answer_text: When I approved the 2025/26 pay uplift in November 2025, the working expectation was that all payments would be made in February 2026. The Business Services Organisation however subsequently advised that whilst it was possible to process payments for salaried staff (including salaried dentists) within this timeline, processing the uplift for General Dental Practitioners was more complex, since it includes a reliance on third-party software providers, with lead-in times set at 12 weeks. This meant that arrears payments for General Dental Practitioners would not be processed until June/July. I have instructed my officials to put in place the necessary arrangements to facilitate an earlier remuneration for General Dental Practitioners, outside of the usual process, for 2025/26. This is a one-off intervention and should not be considered a precedent for future years. I have also directed officials to review the current processes and procedural elements associated with the pay uplift, with a view to improving efficiency for future years’ payments.

answer_text: My Department continues to cooperate with the Department of Education in relation to the acquisition of land owned by my Department which is required to help with the development of a traffic management solution for St Brigid’s College.

answer_text: There is no further update pursuant to the response provided in respect of AQW 27393/22-27. The implementation of Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) at the North West Cancer Centre is included among several service developments aimed at introducing new radiotherapy techniques and technology.  The scale and pace of funding identified will determine the order in which these can be realised.  Due to the lack of identified funding source a specific timeline for the implementation of SABR at the NWCC cannot be provided at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any work undertaken by his Department following the public consultation to complete the Mobuoy Remediation Strategy and implementation plan.
answer_text: The public consultation on the draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site closed on 2 October 2025. Responses have been carefully considered, and the Integrated Consultancy Team appointed by my department, has completed a comprehensive technical evaluation of these responses. The consideration of the responses will now guide the necessary updates to the draft Remediation Strategy document. I am expecting a summary report on the consultation responses to be presented to me, with a view to publishing the report in the coming weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the Public Health Agency business case and implementation plan for the expansion of the bowel screening programme, reducing age eligibility from 60 to 50; and (i) any work undertaken to commence the reduction in sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy 2022-32.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s business case and implementation plan for extending the NI Bowel Screening Programme, from the current 60-74 age eligibility range to 50-74 age eligibility range, has now been received by my department and is currently being considered by officials. The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and to lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. The current UK National Screening Committee recommendation for bowel cancer screening is that it should be offered every 2 years to men and women between the ages of 50 and 74 years old using the FIT test at a sensitivity threshold of 20 µg Hb/g.  Currently in Northern Ireland, men and women aged between 60 and 74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel cancer screening. In April 2023, the NI Bowel Screening Programme did reduce the screening detection FIT threshold to 120 micrograms of blood per gram of stool sample. Work to meet the commitments to expand the bowel screening programme must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Where a Screening Programme expands its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, including diagnostic capacity, workforce and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.I remain committed to ensuring that the NI Bowel Screening Programme is expanded in line with UK National Screening Committee recommendations and as outlined in my three-year HSC Reset Plan, subject to future departmental spending priorities.Regardless of age, anyone currently experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP

answer_text: (a) The information in the table below has been extracted from the Prison Records Information System (PRISM) and details the number of prisoners released in each of the requested calendar years, along with the corresponding number and proportion who had been recorded as having no fixed abode. As a condition of their license, this cohort must reside in accommodation that is approved by their Probation Officer, whether that is in Approved Premises; other temporary accommodation; with family or other co-habiting arrangements; or alone. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she, or her officials, have had with the legal profession in drafting the clauses of the draft Justice Bill, in relation to live links proceedings.
answer_text: The policy for the use of live links for police detention / interviews was the subject of a targeted consultation exercise in April 2020.  The Law Society, Children’s Law Centre and several solicitors’ firms were among the respondents to that exercise.The responses were taken into consideration when drafting the clauses contained within Part 3 of the Justice Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any safeguards in the draft Justice Bill to protect the rights of children and young people, and those with vulnerabilities, in relation to the use of live links.
answer_text: Part 3 of the Justice Bill (the Bill) contains provisions to enable the use of live links by the police.  Clause 20 of the Bill amends Article 40 of the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) to support the use of live links for police interviews.  The clause sets out the roles and responsibilities for officers involved if a live link interview is to take place.  Clause 21 makes provision for the use of live links for extension of detention reviews and hearings for a warrant authorising further detention. These provisions require that the use of a live link is considered appropriate, the person has received legal advice on the use of the live link, and that the appropriate consent has been given.When the Bill receives Royal Assent, amendments will be made to the PACE Codes C, E and F. The purpose of the Code amendments will be to provide safeguards, protections for all participants and guidance for the use of live links in police detention, including police interviews. There will be a public consultation on the proposed Code amendments in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any steps undertaken by his Department to address the lack of car parking at St Brigid's College in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My officials are engaging with the Department for Infrastructure, the Housing Executive and solicitors in relation to acquisition of additional land required to develop a traffic management solution for St Brigid’s College. When acquisition of the land is agreed between the various parties, progress of the scheme will be subject to capital availability and competing priorities.As you will appreciate, the Department is operating within an extremely constrained capital budget and decisions have necessarily been taken to direct available investment to keep schools open, pupils safe and provide education access for the most vulnerable. I will continue to make the case for additional capital funding for education to ensure all our schools are fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is an existing code of practice for the purposes of regulating the use of live links in police interviews.
answer_text: There is no existing code of practice in place regulating the use of live links in police interview.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether a new code of practice needs to be published, in relation to the use of live links in police interviews, for the purposes of protecting the rights and the confidentiality of participants and their legal representatives.
answer_text: Provisions supporting the use of live links in police interviews are contained within Part 3 of the Justice Bill 2024.  When the Bill receives Royal Assent, amendments will be made to the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE) Codes of Practice (Codes).  The purpose of the Code amendments will be to provide safeguards, protections and guidance for the use of live links in police detention, including police interviews.  There will be a public consultation on the proposed Code amendments in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to either dispose of or redevelop the vacant property at Slievemore House, Derry.
answer_text: The wide range of support currently provided by the Department of Health (DoH) to the voluntary and community sectors who deliver primary care health services in the community is directed each year through revenue funding rather than capital grants.The revenue funding includes:The Department’s Elective Care Framework Funding and Implementation Plan includes the Cancer Charities Grant Scheme which involves funding from my Department and match funding support provided by Macmillan Cancer Support.  The Grant Scheme helps support our community, voluntary and charitable sectors in the delivery of cancer support services and projects across local communities.The Elective Care Framework also includes Support While Waiting Grant Scheme which aims to provide meaningful, community-based support to individuals while they await treatment on Health and Social Care (HSC) waiting lists. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of stakeholder engagement with community and voluntary organisations in the design of the 17 Integrated Neighbourhood Models of Care.
answer_text: In the HSC Reset Plan I made a commitment to developing a neighbourhood model of care, to deliver greater levels of care to our citizens in their local communities.  This model will see Community Pharmacy, GPs and their Federations, Voluntary and Community organisations, Trusts and independent providers working closely together, in formal partnerships, to provide integrated care. This is an important enabler for my commitment to ‘Shift Left’.The proposed model will be built around 17 Integrated Neighbourhood Teams acting as provider alliances. These will operate within the Trust/Area Integrated Partnership Board footprint at GP Federation level, serving an average population of 115,000 people. We are taking a 3 phase approach to developing and implementing the Neighbourhood Model.  The first phase (Design) involved wide engagement with a broad range of stakeholders across sectors, including a dedicated engagement event for community and voluntary organisations.  We also ran a Call for Evidence to identify existing good practice. We are about to move into the second phase (Build) to identify neighbourhood sites and activities, to establish systems for delivery and the funding arrangements.  The third phase (Implementation) will commence from April 2026 onwards, when we have worked through the operational details and move into delivery of the programme.We will continue to engage and work closely with all stakeholders, including those in the community and voluntary sector, as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when his Department expects to complete the business case for the planned regional endometriosis centre in the West.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time. In this context, we have secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time report on gynaecology and are progressing the implementation of its recommendations.A Regional Gynaecology Forum was established to take this work forward. The establishment of Regional Endometriosis Centres in the Western Trust and Belfast Trust, along with the enhancement of local services across all five Trusts, is a key priority.All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, with women referred under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive, and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each team member—whether a consultant, nurse, or other professional—functions as a specialist within their discipline. Plans and investment for endometriosis services will commence in 2026. Business Cases relating to both the Regional Centres and local service models are expected to be submitted to the SPPG by March 2026 for consideration.More complex endometriosis care will be provided in the Belfast and Western Trusts, supported by multidisciplinary teams of surgeons, including colorectal and urological consultants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any actions his Department has taken to address NI Environment Agency delays to planning consultation responses.
answer_text: In order to address the delays to planning consultation responses, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) has developed and is implementing a Planning Improvement Plan.  This plan includes a range of measures to improve planning performance and service, and covers 5 strands: technical advice and guidance; caseload management; monitoring and reporting on performance; process and IT systems; and, communication and stakeholder engagement.NIEA continues to work in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which aims to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) a timeline for the completion of her Department's Review of Sentencing Policy and public consultation; and (ii) when responses will be publicly available from her consultation on proposals to criminalise sexually explicit deepfake images.
answer_text: (i) The Review of Sentencing Policy was launched on 14 January 2026, with the aim of considering a range of sentencing policy issues and developing legislative proposals for the next Assembly Mandate.  Subject to resources, it is anticipated that public consultation on policy proposals arising from the Review will take place in 2027.     (ii) Responses to the public consultation which sought views on proposals to criminalise the creation and sharing of sexually explicit deepfake images of adults are currently being analysed and used to inform legislative proposals to be brought forward as an amendment to the Justice Bill at its Consideration Stage.  A summary of the responses and the Department’s way forward will be made publicly available as early as possible in advance of this stage of the Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any progress made with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, on addressing their recent decision to reduce funding for the Economic Activity element of the Local Growth Fund in 2026-2027.
answer_text: I refer the Member to answers provided to previous questions AQW 33970/22-27, AQW 31249/22-27, AQO 2452/22-27, AQO 2458/22-27, AQW 32531/22-27, AQW 33970-22-27 and AQW 36740/22-27. In addition to this on 23 December 2025, I wrote to Minister Fahnbulleh Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State, Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG). In that correspondence, I emphasised the need for greater flexibility and for a funding allocation that better reflects local requirements. I highlighted the detrimental impact that the proposed approach would have on the Community and Voluntary Sector in the North, and I urged Minister Fahnbulleh to reconsider the current profile in favour of one that, at a minimum, aligns with the more favourable Resource profile (44%) afforded to English Mayoral Authorities. To date, I have not had a response.I have also kept my Executive colleagues fully appraised of the situation. This was discussed at the Executive meeting last week, after which, on 22 January, the Executive jointly wrote to the Secretary of State for Housing Communities and Local Government, Steve Reed.  We detailed the devastating impact this recent decision will have. In this letter, we collectively, with one voice, called on Mr Reed to work with his Ministerial colleagues to immediately rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any (i) actions in the last three months; and (ii) future actions planned by his Department, in partnership with the Western Health and Social Care Trust, to improve patient flow across Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has taken a whole-system approach to improving patient flow across Altnagelvin Area Hospital, in partnership with the Western Health and Social Care Trust.In the last three months, actions have included the implementation of the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, published on 16 October 2025. This plan sets out measures to protect services and manage pressures across the system. Within the Western Trust area, specific steps have been taken to improve patient flow and reduce avoidable admissions, including:Eight protected ambulance handover bays at Altnagelvin Emergency Department (ED) open 24/7;Eight additional short stay inpatient beds within the ambulatory care unit at Altnagelvin;Three additional ED consultants appointed in November;12 additional intermediate care beds to facilitate discharge;The expansion of the respiratory ambulatory hub at Altnagelvin to provide a seven-day service; andThe provision of four additional Same Day Emergency Care assessment areas at the South Western Acute Hospital, which will support the ED.To support these initiatives, £2 million has been repurposed for the Western Trust to fund targeted actions across the care pathway, from pre-hospital care through acute care and into the community.Looking ahead, my Department will continue to work with the Western Trust to ensure these measures are fully implemented and sustain improvements in patient flow. Future actions include maintaining enhanced discharge processes, expanding alternative care pathways such as Hospital at Home, and continuing to strengthen collaboration with community and social care partners. These steps are designed to reduce congestion in EDs and improve hospital resilience during periods of peak demand.Collectively, these actions aim to improve patient flow, reduce congestion in EDs, and enhance hospital resilience across the Western Trust during periods of peak demand.My priority remains ensuring timely, safe care for patients while improving system efficiency and resilience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's collaboration with (i) the Department of Transport; (ii) Translink; (iii) Irish Rail; and (iv) the National Transport Authority, to progress a feasibility study for a railway between Derry and Letterkenny.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to exploring opportunities to enhance our rail network and improve sustainable transport options.  A new document was published on 17 December 2025 at www.translink.co.uk/shapingthefuture which outlines a vision for the future development of the North’s rail network. It aligns with the ambitions of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review and presents the key findings from four major project feasibility studies, including the Portadown – Derry line with a connection to Letterkenny. The findings provide a case for investment in a modern and sustainable rail network, subject to the availability of capital and resource funding, as part of a wider network vision to double the number of rail passengers through improved network accessibility, enhanced regional connectivity and inter-urban journey times.  I have also provided £1m of additional funding to maintain momentum on these strategic projects which will allow further route assessment work including the section between Strabane and Derry, which will also consider the connection to Letterkenny.Delivering these four projects, alongside a shift towards rail travel, could unlock significant benefits for the North driving economic growth, promoting social inclusion and supporting environmental sustainability. This progress could also advance our climate ambitions and commitment to achieving net zero emissions. Completion of the feasibility studies is a critical first milestone in defining the projects and their viability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the targets set by his Department for time of arrival to triage at Emergency Departments (EDs); and (ii) any plans his Department has to monitor and review the triage process at EDs.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) a breakdown of the electric vehicle charge point infrastructure, available across the Foyle constituency; and (ii) the future plans for growth, over the next two years.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the position of their current installations and on their plans to install further charge points within their wider networks.With regards to planned installations, Local Councils have availed of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’. This project, part funded by my Department and WEEV, is set to install up to 250 charge points across the North.The Irish Government’s ‘Shared Island Sports Club EV Charging Scheme’ will also be delivering EV chargers at 48 sports clubs in the North bringing further charge points to community settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35046/22-27, to detail (i) any options actively being considered for moving to a second IVF cycle; and (ii) the timeline for the completion of a roadmap for implementation.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any steps his Department is taking to ensure that publicly funded training pathways like the Physician Associates Studies programme at Ulster University are matched with real employment opportunities.
answer_text: My Department works closely with all course providers and employers across the HSC to ensure that posts are available for individuals graduating from the healthcare programmes supported by my Department.In relation to Physician Assistants (PAs) my officials are working proactively with employing Trusts, through the Regional PA Forum they have established, to try to ensure that posts are available for PA graduates despite the significant financial challenges facing the HSC. It is recognised that the identification of recurrent funding is an essential requirement for the creation of jobs for these individuals. My officials will continue to engage with stakeholders on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of; and (ii) the percentage of, publicly funded physician associate graduates who have secured jobs in our health service, in the last five years.
answer_text: There are approximately 80 Physician Assistants (PAs) currently employed across health and social care in Northern Ireland in a range of settings. As detailed in my response to AQW32399/22-27, 74 individuals were employed within Trusts. The information required to answer this specific question is not held by the University nor by Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on the occupational therapy referral waiting list for (i) indoor safety handrails; and (ii) outdoor step safety handrails, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Decisions to recommend indoor or outdoor safety handrails are determined as part of a full comprehensive Occupational Therapy assessment of the referred individual. A recommendation associated with the installation of an internal or external handrail is one of a broad range of potential recommendations which might feature at the conclusion of the full assessment. Data in respect of discrete recommendations would not be routinely collated and as such would not be available.  Handrails are also provided to people who live in public properties, upon request through the Interagency Housing Design Service, these requests do not require an Occupational Therapy assessment.  The HSC Trusts do not have access to the data relating to these requests.

I am also pleased to report that a second phase of development planned for early next year will deliver further teaching spaces, ready for students to start in September of that year.My Department continues to fund additional student places, and the University is confident that enrolments will reach a record high of over 6,300 this year, reflecting strong and sustained growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department has undertaken in the last 12 months to address equitable access to hybrid closed loop technology for people with type 1 diabetes
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.                                                                                                                        I have supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. The implementation plan is currently being drafted and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department has undertaken to progress the development of a national diabetes audit.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of households accessing Home Care Managed Budgets, in each of the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) when the review on Home Care Managed Budgets will be completed
answer_text: Managed Budgets are only in place in some Trust areas due to pilots that were organised in the past to support implementation; in some cases these legacy arrangements have continued. The review of managed budgets commissioned by my Department in partnership with Birmingham University, Impact and the Association for Real Change has indicated that further work is required to support the implementation of Managed Budgets, including standardisation across Trusts in terms of definitions and delivery pathways. At the moment Self Directed Support  in the form of Direct Payments are the key means by which people take control over how their services are delivered.   The figures provided in the below table should be considered mindful of variations in definitions and service delivery models across Trusts. It also should be noted that information held by Trusts with regards to Managed Budgets is in relation to provision across all services, not specifically Home Care Provision.Named of Health and Social Care TrustNumber of households accessing Home Care Managed BudgetsNHSCT13 (further breakdown is unavailable)SHSCT0SET2024/25 -  1362025/26 – 148WHSCT2024/25 - 42025/26 - 13(7 currently remain operational, 6 managed budgets have ceased)BHSCT0The Strategic Planning and Performance Group of my Department partnered with the Association for Real Change and IMPACT, Birmingham University to complete a 12 month review of the current barriers to implementation of Managed Budgets in Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) primary schools; and (ii) secondary schools, that have been successful under the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The purpose of this programme is to target investment in priority areas that will support and enhance delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.I expect to make an announcement in the coming weeks naming the primary schools selected to participate in the pilot for the provision of Outdoor Covered Sports Spaces.	A call has issued recently to all post-primary schools for applications for the provision of Synthetic Pitches, which closes on 24 November 2025.A further call has issued to all primary schools to gauge the level of interest in the provision of Trim Trails, which also closes on 24 November 2025.  Subject to the responses to this call, I may expand my Programme to include the provision of Trim Trails.Defibrillator units are being provided for all schools in Northern Ireland who advised my Department they did not have one. We anticipate these units will be delivered by the end of the calendar year.Additionally, a number of other projects have been delivered under this Programme, including:The refresh of desktop computers to all post-primary and special schools.The Outdoor Learning Project 2025 provided all nursery schools, primary schools and special schools with access to tiered funding allocations, to purchase outdoor learning equipment.The Mobile Phone inhibiting devices pilot which is ongoing in nine schools. A research team has been commissioned to carry out an independent evaluation of the pilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) current; and (ii) planned further, work his Department will undertake, regarding the analysis of submissions to the Consultation on the NI Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy, which closed on 19 September.
answer_text: Work remains ongoing at pace within my Department to drive this vital work forward and my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout this process. This includes input from a wide range of stakeholders including the community and voluntary sector, professional bodies, local government and private individuals. The outcome of this analysis will be shared with all Departments and with my Executive colleagues in due course. Following this, the Executive will decide what further development is required on the Strategy, including any changes deemed appropriate, prior to final publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work undertaken by his Department in the last six months to progress the Cityside Health and Care Centre at Fort George, Derry.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust has completed RIBA Stage 3 for the new Cityside Health and Care Centre Hub at Fort George.  A full planning application was submitted at the end of July 2025 to Derry City and Strabane District Council.Funding to complete the remaining design and deliver the project is subject to finalisation of the Department’s Strategic Capital Development plan, the outcome of which is anticipated early 2026.My ability to commit funding for this project will depend on the Department’s capital allocation from the budget process and the identification of sufficient recurrent funding to meet any additional resource costs associated with the facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to question AQW 32573/22-27, to detail any options currently being considered by the Project Steering Group, to progress the second Ardnashee School and College campus business case.
answer_text: The Project Steering Group is currently considering the options for progression in the Technical Feasibility Report.  I am unable to confirm specific details at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32573/22-27, to detail any consideration that has been given, by the Project Steering Group, to retaining and including the historic Belmont House when progressing the second Ardnashee School and College campus business case.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department has undertaken to progress the canteen refurbishment at St Brigid's College, Derry, in the last 2 years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the provision of school meals at maintained schools pursuant to its obligations contained in Article 58 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.The EA has advised that due to budget constraints and competing priorities, including the need to provide Special Educational Needs placements, there has not been any progress on the canteen refurbishment at St Brigid’s College. 		The schools’ estate needs increased and sustained funding to address the many years of underinvestment, and I will continue to make the case for this additional funding..

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) work required; and (ii) timescale, for the (a) procurement; and (b) delivery, of the approved £4.5m five-class specialist provision extension at St Brigid's College, Derry.
answer_text: The works at St Brigid’s College will deliver a two-storey modular extension to the existing school building which will provide five specialist provision classrooms, pupil and staff toilets, two hygiene rooms, a multipurpose room, sensory room, staff base and office and additional ancillary space; secure external play space will also be provided. As the extension will use a significant portion of the existing hard play area, a new hard play space will be created by removing a sloped grass bank and building a retaining wall.Procurement of an Integrated Design Team (IDT) to carry out detailed design and oversee the project through to delivery will take a minimum of six months.  It is anticipated that the appointment of the IDT will take place in summer 2026.  The current programme for delivery will see construction commence in December 2027 with final delivery in January 2029, however, these dates may be subject to change as they hinge on the receipt of the relevant statutory approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has taken to plan, resource and deliver the New Decade, New Approach commitment to fund three cycles of IVF treatment, in the last two years.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF.In February 2024, my Department increased publicly funded IVF treatment from a partial cycle to one full cycle. Although this expansion was welcomed, it was not possible to expand further due to funding constraints. My Department is continuing to work with the Regional Fertility Centre to explore the feasibility of expanding current capacity from within available resources. This is extremely challenging, as any expansion must be both affordable and deliverable without detriment to current provision and waiting times, which the team has worked hard to maintain after the disruption of the Covid pandemic. While I am cautious about raising expectations at this stage, I am assured that all feasible options for moving to a second cycle are being actively considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made by his Department and the Regional Fertility Centre to maximise the provision of fertility treatment, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF.In February 2024, my Department increased publicly funded IVF treatment from a partial cycle to one full cycle. Although this expansion was welcomed, it was not possible to expand further due to funding constraints. My Department is continuing to work with the Regional Fertility Centre to explore the feasibility of expanding current capacity from within available resources. This is extremely challenging, as any expansion must be both affordable and deliverable without detriment to current provision and waiting times, which the team has worked hard to maintain after the disruption of the Covid pandemic. While I am cautious about raising expectations at this stage, I am assured that all feasible options for moving to a second cycle are being actively considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) phases; (ii) type of access work; and (iii) timescale, for completing the child specific disability access works at St Brigids College, Derry.
answer_text: The minor works scheme to improve accessibility at St Brigid’s College is anticipated to commence in January 2026.  The work will be delivered in phases as some elements will cause significant disruption and are programmed for delivery the during summer terms; this has been agreed with the school.  The whole project is anticipated to be completed by February 2028. The scheme will see interventions in 14 locations throughout the school.  Works include replacement of existing ramps with new, compliant ramps or platform lifts; replacement of a non-compliant lift and replacement with a compliant enclosed platform lift; provision of a hygiene room, accessible toilets and changing rooms; delivery of compliant benches and sinks in specialist classrooms; refurbishment of the medical room; provision of a new fitness area and improvements in accessibility to both the lecture theatre and to the stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Stroke Association documentary Thrombectomy-Restoring Life.
answer_text: The documentary ‘Thrombectomy – Restoring Life’ powerfully demonstrates the impact of thrombectomy on outcomes for stroke survivors. The documentary also identifies the impact of delayed access to thrombectomy and the need for a 24/7 service.This need is recognised in my Department’s Stroke Action Plan which identifies the requirement for a 24/7 service as a priority. However, in the context of the financial pressures facing my Department, I am unable to allocate the additional funding required to support the expanded service at this time. In the interim, I am committed to maximising access to thrombectomy to ensure that as many people as possible benefit from the service within the current hours of operation. To this end, it should be noted that the thrombectomy rate in Northern Ireland in 2024/25 was 5.2%, higher than the regional rates in England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance and support available for people in relation to aftercare once they are discharged under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: It is important that patients arrange any follow-up care, including physio, with their NI treating consultant and local GP before treatment commences. It is recommended that all patients advise their GP and their local Health and Social Care Trust of their intention to travel outside of Northern Ireland for treatment before receiving it. If the patient experience any post-operative complications from surgery they should contact the hospital were the surgery took place in order to get any issues resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department has taken to ensure that those in the north of Ireland who have legally prescribed cannabis-based products for medicinal use do not risk criminalisation when visiting the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health in the Republic of Ireland (ROI) is the competent authority for controlled drugs there and, as such, is responsible for issuance of relevant licences including for import and export.My Department understands that the Department of Health (ROI) policy sets out that prescribed cannabis-based medicinal products are not legally permitted to be brought into the Republic of Ireland.  Any potential change to this would be a matter for the Department of Health (ROI).Enforcement of legislation and operation of criminal justice matters in the Republic of Ireland are not matters for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) what is to be included in a full fiscal framework and (ii) the timeline for its completion.
answer_text: A full Fiscal Framework will consider a number of areas, including the further application of Professor Holtham’s review of our level of need, the borrowing powers of the Housing Executive, and additional fiscal devolution as recommended in the Fiscal Commission report.Negotiating and delivering a full Fiscal Framework will take time and require agreement between the Executive and the British Government. While the Scottish and Welsh Fiscal Frameworks took several years to develop, valuable lessons from their experiences can help expedite this process. I want to make progress as quickly as possible, to ensure the Executive has the funding arrangements and fiscal levers necessary to meet the needs of workers, families and businesses across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the changes in funding that housing associations will receive to construct new homes.
answer_text: New social homes are funded through a combination of grant from my Department and borrowing by the developing Housing Associations, creating a match-funding style arrangement which gives value for the public money invested. My Department regularly updates and reviews cost assumptions and the grant-rates for new Social Housing to ensure that the very significant capital spend by my Department on social housing starts is based on an up-to-date cost assessment. Following careful consideration, I have increased the benchmark costs – the assumed cost of building a new social home – by 13.7% from an average of £181,164 to an average of £206,100. This is an acknowledgement that relevant costs have increased since the benchmark costs and grant rates were last changed in 2023.Rents – which are a source of income to Housing Associations which enables them to repay their borrowing – have also increased. As a result, and in the interest of securing the maximum possible number of new social homes from the Department’s budget, the proportion of grant paid by the Department for Communities for new social housing starts will decrease from an average of 54.2% to an average of 46.52%. This is a 7.7% reduction.The impact of these new rates is, on average, an increased cost to the Housing Association of less than £3k per unit.  It is worth noting that this is the average position and some council areas will see an increase in the amount of grant per unit. To give certainty to the sector, these new benchmark costs and grant rates will be kept in place until the end of the 2026/27 financial year unless there are very exceptional circumstances requiring a change to the rates.I have also announced reviews into how we subsidize new social housing as well as the standards to which they are built.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the areas that still require implementation under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.

To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on his Department’s work to ensure that all Health and Social Care Trust social workers receive mandatory and certified training in the identification of coercive control, trauma, and post-separation abuse as required under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021.
answer_text: The mandatory training provisions under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (NI) 2021 are directed at criminal justice organisations including the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Public Prosecution Service for Northern Ireland and staff within the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunal Service. Although the Act does not extend mandatory training requirements to Health and Social Care Trust social workers, a range of both mandatory and non-mandatory training is available to enhance social workers’ knowledge, skills and confidence in responding to domestic abuse. This training includes topics such as coercive control, trauma and post separation abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to a cross-departmental review with the Department of Justice and the Department of Education on the guidance and interagency protocols on child contact where domestic abuse is a known factor.
answer_text: Article 12A of the Children (NI) Order 1995 currently places a duty on a court, when considering whether to make a residence order or contact order involving a child, to have regard to any domestic abuse offence for which the person seeking contact may have been convicted. The court must also consider whether the person is prohibited by a non-molestation order, whether the child has suffered, or is at risk of suffering, any harm through seeing or hearing ill-treatment of another person by the prohibited person.At this time, no consideration has been given to a cross-departmental review with the Department of Justice and the Department of Education on the guidance and interagency protocols on child contact where domestic abuse is a known factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail their assessment of the recent report from the Simon Community, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, which shows that childhood adversity and trauma drive homelessness here, and which calls for a greater focus on, and investment in, homelessness prevention.
answer_text: The Department welcomes the work done by The Simon Community in Their report ‘Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle’ report (October 2025).  I have already begun to address the issue of investment in homelessness prevention by providing a ringfenced allocation of £2.5m in 2025/26 to the Housing Executive for Strategic Homlessness Prevention activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number; and (ii) type, of options currently being considered by his Department, to progress the second Ardnashee School and College campus business case.
answer_text: A long list of potential options for the second Ardnashee School and College campus was considered at the project initiation meeting on 1 October 2025.  This list is currently being reviewed by the Project Steering Group and has not yet been finalised.  I am therefore unable to confirm definitive options at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on (i) when the works at St Eugene's Primary School, Derry, will commence; and (ii) the estimated timescale for the completion of the works.
answer_text: The business case for the roof work at St Eugene’s Primary School was approved on 1 October 2025. A design team has been appointed and the Education Authority is currently working toward initiating tender for the remedial roof upgrade works. A programme for the works will be provided when a contractor has been appointed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the progression the new paediatric department for Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to allocating capital investment into new youth centre capital projects.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant capital pressures, and there remains no funding available to progress any capital youth schemes.I wrote to the Finance Minister, John O’Dowd MLA, in May 2025, requesting a ringfenced youth capital fund to progress capital projects, but regrettably this was not supported by the Finance Minister.I will continue to make the case for additional and substantial capital investment in our educational infrastructure including youth focused settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) work; or (ii) discussions undertaken with the Education Authority in relation to identifying and addressing the need for new youth facilities.
answer_text: My Department continues to face significant capital pressures, with no funding available to progress any youth capital schemes for both the voluntary and statutory sectors. Given the budgetary pressures, work on new youth facilities is not being taken forward at this time.I will continue to make the case for additional and substantial capital investment in our educational infrastructure including youth focused settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposals, currently being considered by her Department, to secure an acceptable level of protection to homes and businesses in the Eglinton area, Derry, from the various sources of flooding.
answer_text: Development of flood alleviation proposals for Eglinton are currently being progressed to determine if an economically viable solution can be found. The flooding mechanisms in Eglinton are complex with many compounding factors and whilst a potential solution that would mitigate the risk of flooding from the river has been identified, this would not have addressed the flooding witnessed in 2022. Therefore, work is currently progressing to inform a wider-ranging review and explore opportunities for collaboration with other stakeholders regarding some of the interdependencies concerning flood risk at this particular location. This review will look beyond traditional hard engineered solutions and is currently scheduled for completion by Winter 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is on track to complete the detailed design phase of the Drumahoe Flood Alleviation Scheme, by early 2026.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. Consultants are currently preparing an Outline Business Case (OBC) for the scheme and progressing with the detailed design. The current programme indicates that the OBC will be submitted to DoF in spring 2026 with detailed design work to be completed by the end of 2026. Procurement of a contractor to deliver the scheme may commence in 2027, subject to ongoing viability.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the next phase of the North West Regional Development Fund.
answer_text: Engagement on the future support for the North West Structures and the focus of the North West Development Fund is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department has taken to encourage more females into trades.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy recognises that women remain underrepresented in many trade sector apprenticeships and is actively working to address this imbalance through a range of targeted initiatives. One key intervention is the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund, which supports projects aimed at removing barriers and creating inclusive pathways into apprenticeships. This includes programmes such as #NotJustForBoys Pathways+, delivered by Women’s Tec, which specifically encourages and supports women to enter traditionally male-dominated trades. This project will address the significant under-representation of women in industries such as construction, engineering and mechanics, particularly within apprenticeships through a holistic, cross-sectoral approach. The introduction of All Age Apprenticeships in September 2023 has provided flexible entry points for individuals of all ages, helping more women return to the workforce and consider careers in skilled trades. In addition, the Department is increasing the visibility of female apprentices in promotional materials to challenge stereotypes and encourage broader participation. Careers Advisers also play a key role by providing impartial advice and guidance, encouraging individuals to explore all career options and actively challenge gender stereotypes in occupational choices. These actions are underpinned by the Department’s recently published three-year Apprenticeship Action Plan, which includes a specific focus on inclusion, ensuring that all sectors of society, including women, are supported to access and benefit from apprenticeship opportunities. While gender representation in apprenticeships often reflects wider employment trends, the Department remains committed to promoting diversity and inclusion across all apprenticeship pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration made to commissioning a dedicated multi-disciplinary team of surgeons for more complex care for patients with endometriosis.
answer_text: My officials established a Regional Gynaecology Forum last year.  This forum has set up a number of clinically led Task and Finish groups.  The Clinically Led Endometriosis Task and Finish Group have developed the most appropriate pathway for women with endometriosis considering extant standards, clinical guidelines and best practice.  The pathway will take account of the most appropriate treatments for the condition at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care level, depending on the nature and severity of condition.Currently the treatment of endometriosis is available in all Gynaecology Departments at secondary care level across Northern Ireland, however, treatment for severe endometriosis will only be available in Belfast and Western Trust, following accreditation of these regional centres.The accreditation of endometriosis centres is a critical aspect of providing high-quality care for patients with this complex condition.  The British Society for Gynaecological Endoscopy (BSGE) accredits centers where gynaecologists work in appropriate clinical teams, audit their outcomes, and maintain surgical skills.  The requirements for BSGE accreditation include:Consultant Gynaecologists with appropriate specialist training and expertise, which is available inNorthern Ireland;Endometriosis Clinical Nurse Specialists (CNSs) with dedicated protected time and participation in professional development which will need expanded across Northern Ireland;Centres must have sufficient workload to maintain surgical skills and provide comprehensive care for severe endometriosis; and,A wraparound multidisciplinary service also needs to be in place, including access to specialist colorectal and urology consultants for complex theatre surgeries; with access to pain, fertility and psychological services.These accredited centres will offer complex surgery for those with suspected or confirmed severe endometriosis, ensuring that patients receive the best possible care, by experts in their field.  The BSGE accreditation process is designed to maintain high quality standards of care and ensure that patients have access to specialised treatment.There are currently 75 BSGE accredited centres in England, 3 in Wales and 4 in Scotland.Securing the right service for Northern Ireland is part of the work that will be achieved as part of the Regional Gynaecology Forum and recurrent investment in 2025/6 and 2026/7 is required to develop pathways for  local and tertiary services.
