question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the outcome of the Education Authority survey of all preschool settings in early 2025, to refresh the self-assessed state of readiness data of individual settings for cohort two of the Pre-School Education Programme Standardisation Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority has completed its analysis of the data gathered through its survey on the state of readiness of pre-school settings carried out in Spring of this year and has been liaising closely with settings to validate the information provided by settings in their survey returns to enable them to identify the next cohort of potential settings to transition to full-time provision from September 2026. It is my intention to transition approximately 2,200 additional full-time places or circa 100 settings from September 2026 and the Education Authority has recently forwarded the first Tranche of 33 settings that have been identified as potential candidates and have requested to transition from September 2026.  Consultation on these settings was launched on 25 September 2025, details of which can be found here.  Consultation on further settings identified by the Education Authority for transition in September 2026 will follow in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when his Department plans to consult with individual settings, being considered under the Pre-School Education Programme Standardisation Programme, for approval to transition from September 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority has completed its analysis of the data gathered through its survey on the state of readiness of pre-school settings carried out in Spring of this year and has been liaising closely with settings to validate the information provided by settings in their survey returns to enable them to identify the next cohort of potential settings to transition to full-time provision from September 2026. It is my intention to transition approximately 2,200 additional full-time places or circa 100 settings from September 2026 and the Education Authority has recently forwarded the first Tranche of 33 settings that have been identified as potential candidates and have requested to transition from September 2026.  Consultation on these settings was launched on 25 September 2025, details of which can be found here.  Consultation on further settings identified by the Education Authority for transition in September 2026 will follow in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications made to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group for the use of relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone for treating symptoms of endometriosis in adults of reproductive age who have had medical or surgical treatment for endometriosis, since May 2025, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: No applications have been submitted to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group for the use of relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone in the treatment of endometriosis symptoms since May 2025. This medication has been approved for hospital prescribing for women who meet the criteria and has been added as a stock item by pharmacy procurement in line with NICE guidance. Therefore, Trusts are able to prescribe relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone for the treatment of endometriosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the PitStop initiative between Altnagelvin Area Hospital and the NI Ambulance Service for those presenting with stroke symptoms.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm the PITSTOP pilot involving NIAS and Western HSC Trust remains in place to optimise the number of patients who can available of thrombectomy and reduce the time from arrival at Altnagelvin until transfer to the Royal Victoria Hospital. Sixty patients have been pre-alerted by NIAS and received rapid multi-disciplinary assessment in Altnagelvin since the pilot commenced in June 2024. Of these, seven patients were eligible and transferred to the Royal Victoria Hospital for thrombectomy using the same ambulance which issued the pre-alert.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progression of the second campus for Ardnashee School and College, Foyle.
answer_text: An Integrated Consultancy Team has been appointed to take forward the project for the second campus of Ardnashee School and College.  A Project Initiation meeting took place on Wednesday 1 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what actions his Department is taking to ensure a full staff complement each night at the NI Ambulance Emergency Operations Control Centre.
answer_text: I would like to acknowledge the outstanding dedication of staff in the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) Emergency Operations Control Centre (EOC). These professionals deliver a high-quality service under extremely challenging circumstances, often supporting people at the most critical moments of their lives. The Northern Ireland EOC is officially recognised as an Accredited Centre of Excellence, reflecting the consistently high standards of care and professionalism demonstrated by the team.My Department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards through the development of a 10-year workforce and financial plan.  To ensure a full complement of staff each night, NIAS is strengthening workforce capacity within the EOC. A structured training programme is underway, with seven new emergency medical dispatchers, expected to qualify in October, and a further intake of ten staff scheduled for training in November. These steps are part of a broader strategy by NIAS to enhance operational resilience, particularly in preparation for winter pressures.In addition, reciprocal arrangements with other UK ambulance services are in place to provide mutual support during periods of exceptional demand or staff shortages. These arrangements ensure that 999 calls can be answered promptly and triaged using standard protocols, helping to maintain continuity of service.My Department remains committed to ensuring safe and effective staffing levels in the EOC and will continue working closely with NIAS to monitor and respond to emerging needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on what work his Department has completed collaboratively with the Department of Justice to address drug-related and drug-misuse deaths.
answer_text: The Executive’s Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’ has a vision that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, have access to high quality treatment and support services, and will be empowered to maintain recovery.Alongside its other priorities, the strategy recognises the importance of reducing availability of, and accessibility to, illegal substances and we work collaboratively with the PSNI, the Organised Crime Taskforce Drug sub-group, and the Department of Justice in relation to several key actions in this respect.The Department of Justice plays a vital role on the Substance Use Strategy Programme Board which oversees the Strategy bringing forward current and emerging drug related issues for discussion to help inform ongoing policy development and advice which is fed into the implementation process.In respect of the action in the Substance Use Strategy on how to reduce violence, or the threat of violence, towards drug users from some paramilitary and vigilante groups, the Public Health Agency were commissioned by the NI Executive programme for tackling paramilitary activity/organised crime and the Department of Health to undertake a scoping for drug related intimidation (DRI) across Northern Ireland. Extensive engagement/research with affected individuals and their families, staff in a range of sectors (justice, health/substance use, housing, youth work, etc), and the general population has resulted in five research reports and an overarching summary report with a proposed response model, which will be published in the near future.Additionally, my Department oversees the Drug & Alcohol Monitoring & Information System (DAMIS) with support from lead partners the Public Health Agency, the Department of Justice, the Police Service of Northern Ireland, and Forensic Science Northern Ireland. DAMIS is an “early warning system” designed to find out about emerging trends in drug use, so we can act quickly and provide relevant information or advice to those who misuse substances. The kind of information DAMIS collects includes: sudden increases in the use of a particular drug; drugs being consumed in new ways; new drugs becoming available; and/or the emergence of substances with unexpected unpleasant or dangerous effects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work her Department has undertaken collaboratively with the Department of Health to strengthen statutory provision of harm reduction measures.
answer_text: While the statutory responsibility for supporting those with addictions falls to the Department of Health (DoH), my department is committed to improving outcomes for those in contact with the justice system through a variety of interventions and collaborative mechanisms. This includes:DoH commissioned the South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust (the Trust) to provide primary care, mental health and addiction services to people living in prison.  Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) staff work closely with Trust staff including the delivery of joint strategies for the reduction of the harm caused by substance use within prison, as well as suicide and self-harm risk management. In addition, a joint approach to the support of people who may be at risk of suicide or serious self-harm was introduced in 2018. The joint approach Supporting People at Risk Evolution was reviewed in 2024/25.In addition to having specialist addictions staff within the healthcare teams at each establishment, the Trust has commissioned Start 360 to provide drug counselling and therapeutic interventions on its behalf.  Information has also been made available to prisoners on release, including contact numbers in the community, to raise awareness around the safe use of substances.  In order to deter the availability of illicit drugs within prisons, NIPS has introduced a range of measures aimed at disrupting supply including x-ray body scanners.The Department of Justice has contributed to the development of the Department of Health’s 10-year Substance Use Strategy and officials continue to be engaged in the implementation of relevant actions.  Both Departments have also progressed work through the Health in Justice Strategy and action plan.To improve outcomes for those in police custody, a police custody healthcare model was introduced into Musgrave custody suite in 2018 and has since been rolled out in Antrim and Waterside custody suites.  This nurse-led model ensures that detainees have access to appropriate interventions and support, including in relation to addictions and mental ill-health, whilst in police custody.  Custody nurse practitioners have also established referral pathways to a number of organisations such as Lifeline, Extern and Addiction NI, which can assist vulnerable individuals post-custody to be signposted to appropriate services.Belfast PCSP contributes to two significant projects within Belfast which aim to tackle the harms and vulnerabilities associated with substance use. Firstly, the Street Injecting Support Service which targets the impact of discarded injecting equipment within the city limits, with a focus on the city centre and delivers proactive outreach in Belfast city centre to provide a flexible response to need including sign posting to other support services, as well as providing highly visible reassurance to the public and City Centre business owners. The PCSP also contribute to the Complex Lives model which has been developed to provi

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on progressing proposals in respect of the development of a Barnahus model.
answer_text: The Barnahus Working Group, jointly chaired by Departments of Justice and Health, held a workshop in June 2025 to consider an options paper for a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland.  Subsequently, a programme of wider stakeholder engagement has been undertaken and remains ongoing.Officials in both Departments are also currently working to identify high level costs based on the preferred option identified by the working group.  I understand that a final options paper will be presented to both the Health Minister and me for consideration in due course.Implementation of any model will be subject to appropriate funding and resources being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any actions his Department has taken to (i) seek clarity on how the Local Growth funding which replaced the UK Shared Prosperity Fund will operate here; and (ii) update local organisations that will be impacted.
answer_text: The UK Government has announced details of a new Local Growth Fund to support regional economic development from 2026/27 to 2028/29. Northern Ireland will receive Local Growth Fund support at the same cash level as its 2025/26 SPF allocation, £45 million per year. 		The fund will be delivered in partnership between the Northern Ireland Office (NIO) and the Northern Ireland Executive, though the exact structure of this partnership is still being developed. The Finance Minister continues to engage with the UK Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government and the NIO on this fund and a trilateral Ministerial meeting is planned to discuss the delivery of this funding in NI.		This Department has identified our priorities for the Local Growth Fund and continues to fund and collaborate with Voluntary and Community sector organisations in the delivery of a wide range of bespoke employability provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the work of the subgroups that report to the Right Care Right Person Silver Operational Group.
answer_text: There have been 9 subgroups established which report to the Right Care Right Person (RCRP) Silver Operational Group. Each subgroup, listed below, is considering the implications and preparing for the introduction of RCRP.Interagency working arrangementsInteragency Memorandums of Understanding (MoUs) are being developed for Welfare Checks, Gaining Entry, Articles 129/130 of the Mental Health (NI) Order 1986 and Conveyance.  The MoU for Articles 129/130 will build on the revised Mental Health Order Code of Practice which is due to go out for consultation by the end of September 2025. It will also cover patients who are detained under the Mental Health Order who go absent without leave.Use of RestraintThis group has recently been established, with a focus on clarifying the level of restraint that can be used by each of the organisations involved in the implementation of RCRP. Acute Care NetworkThis group has recently been established, however it covers issues that are wider than RCRP.  The purpose of the group is to review and understand the causes of protracted delays in relation to acute psychiatric care and to consider solutions for addressing protracted waits and enabling timely access to bed-based care within a regionally agreed timeframe.  This will support the implementation of RCRP.Places of SafetyA draft options paper has been developed outlining potential options for designated Places of Safety. Initial options have been developed to improve support for ASWs and a workshop is planned for later in the year to agree on the preferred model.Medical Assessments CapacityAn options appraisal paper has been developed with two potential options for how capacity in relation to medical assessments can be maximised.  This subgroup will present their findings to the Silver Group in October 2025.Evaluation/OutcomesThis subgroup is currently being established and will be co-chaired by the Department of Health and PSNI. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any other funding opportunities his Department are exploring in 2025-26 to support those football clubs and organisations that were unsuccessful under the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: It is my intention that the Northern Ireland Football Fund will be a rolling programme and that those applicants not in the prioritised cohort will be brought forward to a further stage of the Performance Programme if more funding is made available. I will be exploring all possible avenues to secure this funding including from within my own Departmental budget and the use of Financial Transactions Capital.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department set and agreed the number of successful applications under each tier of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: When deciding how many projects to take forward from each tier in rank order, I considered a number of options presented by my officials. Based on this advice, I opted to take forward a range of small, medium and large projects to ensure that the available budget can be utilised to maximise benefits for football and communities and avoid any further erosion of its value.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department factored in the Programme for Government priority of regional balance into the decision-making process regarding Northern Ireland Football Fund stadia funding.
answer_text: This was a fair and transparent process. All applicants had an equal opportunity to make their case for why their project should be taken forward first.Well in advance of the call for applications a Rural Needs Impact Assessment) (RNIA)was completed on The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF) Performance Programme. This assessment recognised that TNIFF’s benefits were expected to span a wide range of Programme for Government themes but also noted that TNIFF was not intended to disburse funding in such a way as to secure a regional spread; rather all applications would be subject to the same scoring matrix with no advantage or disadvantage given to rural clubs. Decisions were therefore taken on an assessment of need and merit rather than to achieve geographical spread. There is no evidence that need is equally distributed across all geographic areas. The RNIA was published on the Department for Communities website in October 2024. A copy can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-northern-ireland-football-fund-performance-club-strand-rnia.PDFA copy of both the scoring matrix and applicant guidance notes  were published in January 2025 and can be found at Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme documents | Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's plans to support those football clubs and organisations that will not progress at this stage with funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: The clubs not selected in the prioritised cohort have been offered the opportunity to contact officials to arrange a meeting to discuss their applications and receive feedback. It is hoped that these clubs will be brought forward to a further stage of the Programme if additional funding is secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a winter preparedness plan for 2025-26 to improve patient flow and reduce waiting times at emergency departments.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps her Department has taken with the Minister of Health and the Minister for Communities to eradicate the use of drugs at concerts and events.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 6 - AQO 2361/22-27		  ANSWER May I first extend my deepest condolences to the families and friends of the two young people who tragically lost their lives recently, my thoughts are with all those affected at this heartbreaking time.  I work closely with the Health Minister on drug related issues and we recently attended a UK Drugs Ministerial meeting where there was a wide-ranging discussion on enforcement, prevention, treatment and recovery. My Department is a member of the Health-led Substance Use Strategy Programme Board along with DfC, DfI and DE, helping to drive forward and oversee the implementation of the 10-year strategy to tackle the harms caused by substance use. There are several actions within the strategy that my Department is either directly or indirectly involved in delivering.  My officials also sit on the TEO-led Safer Socialising Steering Group, together with DfC and HSCNI representatives, which seeks to develop measures to keep people safer whilst socialising, with a specific consideration for women and girls.  My department, through the work of the Organised Crime Task Force, which includes representatives from DoH, also supports the ongoing efforts of law enforcement partners to tackle drugs in our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her three-pronged approach to wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: This approach has proven to be very successful with considerable progress in all three areas.FundingWith regard to funding, this year my Department has provided NI Water with over £500m of public money this year to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North. When the additional funding that I secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, NI Water is now receiving 90% of the capital funding it had identified it could deliver this year, representing a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.In addition, since Autumn 2024, my Department has been able to unlock wastewater capacity to enable future wastewater connections for 5,300 new homes, through the allocation of an additional £30.8m to NI Water from October and June monitoring. This exceeds NI Water’s own target of unlocking 4,500 properties in this current Price Control.My Department has also been successful in securing £15m over the next four years from the Executive’s Transformation Project to take forward an Urban Drainage Transformation Project. The aim of this project is to retrofit a range of nature-based drainage solutions into the urban environment to manage rainwater on the surface and slow its flow into our rivers and sewers. Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill My Department’s Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill is currently scheduled for its Second Stage Debate tomorrow and, assuming the Bill passes Second Stage, the Committee Stage will commence immediately afterwards.Following the completion of the remaining stages and Royal Assent, I anticipate the legislation coming into operation in 2026. Developer ContributionsOn Developer Contributions for wastewater infrastructure, the public consultation has now closed and almost 300 responses were received. This feedback will be invaluable in shaping future decisions and a consultation response paper will issue in due course. I will continue to do all that I can to enable the release of wastewater capacity constraints to support future housing and wider economic development.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 27776/22-27, for an update on the engagement with the Irish Government to progress and invest in the next phase of the North West Regional Development Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department can take to prevent the closure of the Community Crisis Intervention Service, Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have been red-flagged by their GP and are currently waiting for a medical investigation appointment at a breast clinic, in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by the length of wait in weeks.
answer_text: A regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment became operational on 8 May 2025. The current regional wait for breast assessment is 8 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when breast clinic services became regional.
answer_text: Breast Clinical Services have not been regionalised.  However, my Department implemented a regional waiting list for red flag breast cancer assessment on 8 May 2025. This initiative is part of a broader review of Breast Services and is designed to ensure that all patients across Northern Ireland have access to the earliest available appointment, regardless of their home Trust.Most individuals will continue to be seen at their nearest hospital; however, where earlier appointments are available, patients will be offered an appointment at any of the acute hospitals. Following assessment, patients are referred back to their home Trust for treatment if required.

To ask the Minister of Health whether Pharmacy First Sore Throat Test and Treat will be extended beyond August 2025.
answer_text: The Pharmacy First Pilot Service for Sore Throat in patients aged 5 years and over ran between November 2023 and March 2024 in 43 community pharmacies in NI. Following the successful evaluation of the service, the service was offered to all community pharmacies in December 2024, as part of Winter Planning 24/25 followed by a recent extension until 31 August 2025. Decisions about the continuation of the service as part of the Winter Plan for 25/26 will be made in due course, considering service outcomes and funding. My Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan, developed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland. Work is ongoing to finalise the commissioning plan for 2025-2028 and outline the service priorities for the next three years, aligned with the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan which was launched by my Department in May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's plan to introduce new and additional Pharmacy First services.
answer_text: Within the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030 one of the priorities is to expand the range of clinical services available through community pharmacies to improve access to patient care pathways for the diagnosis, treatment and prevention of illness. This will include the Pharmacy First Service; further development of this service in Northern Ireland is dependent on having an electronic platform and the necessary funding. My Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan, developed with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland. Work is ongoing to finalise the commissioning plan for 2025-2028 and outline the service priorities for the next three years, aligned with the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan which was launched by my Department in May 2024. Pharmacy First service for Everyday Health Conditions has been operating in Northern Ireland since March 2022, replacing the previous Minor Ailments Service which had been in place since 2010.  All community pharmacies are contracted to provide this service and 166,382 consultations took place in 2023/24. In addition, the Pharmacy First service has expanded in recent years to include a number of new clinical areas as follows; The Pharmacy First Service for Emergency Hormonal Contraception (EHC) was introduced in July 2022 The Pharmacy First Service for Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs) was added to the service in November 2023. The Pharmacy First Sore Throat Test and Treat Service for management of sore throat symptoms was brought forward as part of the Winter Plan 24/25; inclusion on the 25/26 plan will be subject to review of service outcomes and funding The Pharmacy First Treatment of Shingles Pilot was launched on 6th January 2025 in 50 pharmacies across Northern Ireland. This will be subject to full evaluation before considering if it should continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the publication of a progress report on the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to reduce the test sensitivity threshold for the bowel cancer screening programme in line with the UK National Screening Committee recommendations.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and to lowering the age range to 50 years of age in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. In April 2023, the NI Bowel Screening Programme reduced the screening detection threshold to 120 micrograms of blood per gram of stool sample. Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range and further reduce the sensitivity level is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. It is important to note that screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity, workforce and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range and further reduce the sensitivity level is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. It is important to note that screening is a pathway, not just a ‘test.’ As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity, workforce and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24245/22-27, for an update on the commissioned regional hospital bed demand and capacity, including inpatient bed requirements and availability.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing a regional demand and capacity exercise, which includes a comprehensive assessment of inpatient bed requirements and availability across the Health and Social Care (HSC) system. This work is being undertaken to inform future service planning and ensure that hospital bed capacity is aligned with projected demand.As part of this initiative, a proposal has been approved to conduct an evidence-based review of unscheduled care demand and capacity, following recommendations from the Unscheduled and Emergency Care Review and the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.  There are a number of pieces of work which have already been completed or are in progress which will inform this work.As part of the GIRFT Review, a Summary Emergency Department Indicator Table (SEDIT) was developed for Northern Ireland showing four pillars: demand, capacity, flow and outcomes. The SEDIT is updated every month and enables benchmarking of the 10 Emergency Departments (EDs) in Northern Ireland against sites in England and Wales. The SEDIT consistently shows that demand and capacity compares favourably in NI against other parts of the UK.  Capacity within the SEDIT looks at a range of factors including ED and acute hospital bed capacity. Demand looks at ED attendances, ambulance arrivals and hospital admissions. The SEDIT also clearly shows that flow and outcomes are poor in hospitals in Northern Ireland, highlighting the need to continue to focus on improving patient flow and reducing delays in our hospitals, especially around discharge.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working with the Public Health Agency (PHA) to develop a population health model which will take factors including demographic changes and disease prevalence, to help better understand how the demand profile for our population will change over the next ten years. As this model is being developed in-house, it should allow us to continually refine and update the planning assumptions to help inform decisions about how we plan services.  The PHA are aiming to have an initial model available in early July which will then be refined in the coming months.Work is also being finalised with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) to develop a comprehensive 10-year review of capacity and demand to inform the clinical workforce requirements for the service. This work will have implications on how NIAS will develop as a service and respond to demand, which will in turn impact on hospital demand.A Patient Flow Data Group has been established to support the eight projects borne from ‘The Big Discussion’ process to help the system better manage pressures in winter 2025/26 and into the years ahead. The SPPG is participating in this work and will consider how the data produced can also support the demand capacity review.Therefore, at this time my Department does not currently hold the specific hospital-level data requested. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the future of the Urban Villages Initiative.
answer_text: We recognise the significant and positive impact the current Urban Villages programme has made within communities. The successful completion of the remaining capital projects will further expand access to shared spaces and continue to foster meaningful integration across the five Urban Villages areas and beyond.We have clearly defined the strategic direction for the Urban Villages initiative, which includes:Completing the current Urban Villages plan, with a strong emphasis on delivering the remaining capital projects; andEnsuring the long-term sustainability of these investments by supporting community organisations that are leading on the major capital developments.We are actively planning the next phase of the Urban Villages programme and will provide Members with a comprehensive update once the details have been finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress made by her Department and the developer to complete an agreed detailed design for the upgrade and signalisation of Caw roundabout, Derry.
answer_text: Following detailed considerations, I am advised that my officials have given approval to the alternative mitigation strategy put forward by the developer, South Bank Square. The alternative proposals will see the Caw Roundabout upgrade delivered sooner, with the works to be in place prior to occupation of more than 262 dwellings. In addition, the upgrade is time-bound and must be completed prior to the end of November 2026.My officials continue to work with the developer to progress the detailed design of the Caw Roundabout upgrade. The preliminary design and design parameters have been agreed and it is anticipated that the detailed design will be agreed later this year, to enable the works to commence on site in early 2026.This should provide a significant improvement in both road safety and traffic progression on the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of consultant psychologists specialising in addiction available to Community Addiction Teams, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following information has been provided by each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts.The Belfast Trust Community Addiction Team does not have Consultant Psychology Provision.In the Northern HSC Trust, there is currently 0.4 WTE of Consultant Psychologist aligned to the Community Addictions Service. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on progressing and implementing the Right Care Right Person initiative, in partnership with Department of Health.
answer_text: As I have previously indicated the Right Care Right Person initiative is an operational matter for the PSNI.  My Department alongside the DoH and other stakeholders, is seeking to assist the PSNI in the development of RCRP by providing a joint multi-agency co-ordination role alongside the Silver Group (the key decision making group) in the development, implementation and monitoring of the RCRP approach in Northern Ireland;  and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of mental health care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update in progressing and implementing the Right Care Right Person initiative in partnership with Department for Justice.
answer_text: My officials are liaising closely with counterparts in the Department of Justice and PSNI and have agreed appropriate governance structures to ensure a coordinated approach to the future implementation of Right Care Right Person in NI.  A RCRP Strategic Group has been established, chaired jointly by senior officials in Justice and Health, working with senior representatives from PSNI, and health and social care.  The first meeting of this group was held on 29 October 2024 and provided an opportunity to discuss the scope and membership of the Group, with a further meeting held on 15 April 2025.  The terms of reference for the Strategic Group are still under review and it is anticipated that they will soon be finalised.  As part of governance arrangements, the Strategic Group agreed that a National Partnership Agreement (NPA) should be developed which will be signed off by the key agencies involved in the roll out of the RCRP model.  The development of the NPA is underway.  A ‘Silver’ Operational Group to take forward the implementation of RCRP, co-chaired by the PSNI and Department of Health, had its first meeting in December 2024.  Further meetings have been held in February 2025, April 2025 and most recently on 10 June 2025. A number of subgroups which report to the RCRP Silver Group have been established and include key stakeholders from across health and justice.  Work continues to be progressed at subgroup level.As the work progresses it is anticipated that this will highlight what changes will be needed within the HSC system to support implementation of RCRP and whether additional funding will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the provision for the use of cold caps for cancer patients during treatment (i) in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) at the North West Cancer Centre at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: Cold caps/scalp cooling treatment is not routinely commissioned in Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland.  Cold cap treatment  is  not part of the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommended care pathway for treatment of chemotherapy/Systemic anti-cancer therapy (SACT) induced hair loss.  The routine commissioning of this treatment can only be considered based on the long-term evidence of efficacy, available funding and the relative priority of this service in the context of other service pressures and service development proposals, and the overall HSC financial position.The commissioning position on Scalp Cooling can be reviewed on receipt of any emerging NICE recommendations for cancer patients. The NI Cancer Network (NICaN) SACT Clinical Reference Group, comprising regional and multi-professional members, reviewed the literature and evidence base in January 2018 and again in April 2022. Besides efficacy concerns, scalp cooling treatment alongside SACT would significantly impact SACT chair time. Given the current system pressures to meet demand and deliver essential SACT services across all units in Northern Ireland, the group consensus was not to prioritise the introduction of Scalp Cooling over other SACT treatment needs at this time. The group continues to review any new scalp cooling evidence as it emerges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she proposes to adequately ensure that children are considered as a distinct rights group within the Justice Bill.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 - AQO 2076/22-27		  ANSWER Provisions in the Justice Bill which are specific to children, or may have an impact on children, have been carefully developed and drafted to ensure that the best interests of a child are fully considered and taken account of when decisions are being made.  This includes building safeguards into provisions for children where necessary and including clauses to ensure specific approaches are taken when children are involved.  The youth justice provisions in the Justice Bill have been developed with a view to enhancing the Department’s compliance with international obligations, in particular, Article 37 of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child, which addresses the use of custody for children.  The Department believes that all youth justice policies and services should be child-centred.  To that end, the relevant youth justice provisions have been developed to ensure that the best interests of the child are considered when decisions are made in relation to bail, remand and custody of children.    This child-centred approach is demonstrated through the proposal to make bail for children a statutory right, to have unconditional bail as standard and to ensure that children are never held in adult custodial facilities and are subject to child-appropriate sentencing arrangements where possible.  An equality impact assessment screening exercise was undertaken in respect of the youth justice provisions in the Justice Bill and only identified positive impacts for children and young people.  The Department has engaged with the Human Rights Commission and the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People whilst drafting provisions throughout the Bill.  We are also working closely with the Justice Committee to provide full and detailed answers to any queries they receive relating to children and young people during its Call for Evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant of AQW 26130/22-27, to detail (i) the projected annual cost for staffing, including associated training costs, for the delivery of Sterotactic Ablative Radiotherapy at North West Cancer Centre; and (ii) the expected timeline for implementation.
answer_text: (i) As referenced in AQW 26130/22-27, the North West Cancer Centre (NWCC) has the necessary equipment to implement Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) however delivery of this service is dependent on funding for additional staff.  It is estimated that the annual cost of staffing to deliver SABR in the NWCC could be in the region of £284,000 to recruit the necessary therapeutic radiographers and medical physicist required to support the service.(ii) The implementation of SABR at the NWCC is included among several service developments aimed at introducing new radiotherapy techniques and technology.  The scale and pace of funding identified will determine the order in which these can be realised.  Due to the lack of identified funding source a specific timeline for the implementation of SABR at the NWCC cannot be provided at this time.

answer_text: The Final Budget for 2025-26 was agreed by the Executive on 3 April 2025. Following my announcement to the Executive on 2 June, I continue to acknowledge the vital role provided by the Voluntary and Community Sector (VCS). These organisations create an enormously important social value, through community engagement, job creation, and overall improvement in people's quality of life. Given the rising cost pressures facing this sector I was therefore delighted to announce on 2 June, that within my Department’s initial allocations 2025/26 to the NI Assembly, I proposed to increase revenue support to the VCS by £2.8M. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what specialised services are currently commissioned to treat women suffering from endometriosis.
answer_text: Identifying the number of endometriosis specialists in place cannot be confined to a single profession, as this is not how care is provided and would be outwith best practice. All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, as women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  Gynaecology Services are provided through multidisciplinary teams which are best to ensure holistic, responsive, appropriate patient care throughout their journey.  Each member of the team functions as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, or other professional lead in delivering care. A recent development, on 16 April, in the treatment of endometriosis can be seen in the NICE published TA1057 which recommends the use of relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone for treating symptoms of endometriosis in adults of reproductive age who have had medical or surgical treatment for endometriosis. I am pleased to advise that this treatment is now available locally in line with the criteria set out in NICE TA1057 via the process for the Managed Entry of New Medicines on a cost-per-case basis. Individuals requiring access to treatment should discuss the matter with their hospital consultant in the first instance who will make a cost-per-case application to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within my Department, if appropriate.More complex care for patients with endometriosis, will be provided in Belfast and Western Trusts, with care being provided by a multi-disciplinary team of surgeons including colorectal and urological consultants. My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time.  In this regard my Department has secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time Report on gynaecology and progressing implementation of its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Central Good Relations Fund.
answer_text: The Central Good Relations Fund (CGRF) supports constituted voluntary and community sector groups to deliver good relations projects.We recognise the vital work undertaken by community organisations through the CGRF and are delighted to have supported nearly 900 projects to date, benefitting almost 290,000 people.The Central Good Relations Fund is extremely competitive with demand always exceeding available funding. For 2025/26 we received 220 applications from groups, requesting a total funding of £7.1m. The opening budget for 2025/26 has been confirmed at £2.025 million. All applicants were advised on the outcome of their applications on 17 April 2025 and delivery has now commenced.A total of 62 successful projects have been awarded funding which will positively impact on almost 40,000 people. A further 49 groups remain on a waiting list should additional budget become available during the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to BT's decision to close its office in Foyle, for an update on the impact of this decision on jobs.
answer_text: On 30 April 2025 BT announced, that as part of a global review of its estate provision and operations, that it intended to commence a process of consultation with staff and their trade union representatives regarding the future of operations at its site in Derry. BT has advised that this may lead to the transfer and cessation of two main desk-bound functions at the site: Business Service and Group Business Services (GBS).Subject to this consultation, which is now under way, BT has advised that up to 140 staff at the Derry site are affected by the announcement which could lead to the transfer of 47 positions to its Belfast Riverside Tower facility and up to 90 other posts to India. If fully implemented this proposal would see the BT building in Derry close to all such desk-based staff by 31 December 2025. I understand that the building may however remain open to continue to house telecommunications equipment and serve as a base for field engineering operations in the region.The news of the threat to BT jobs in Derry is deeply disappointing, especially coming so soon after the loss of 300 jobs in BT EE in Enniskillen last year. As a significant employer in Derry for many years, this announcement will be of real concern to BT workers and the wider local community.Work has already commenced with the company seeking to secure the best possible outcome for those staff affected. I met with the company on the 30th April, joined by INI officials, where I made clear to BT that these plans are entirely contrary to my drive for regional balance. Invest NI will continue to work with BT management and other stakeholders in the region in the months to come to seek to affect the best possible outcome for staff concerned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration that has been given to resource an acute oncology haematology ambulatory care service at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: The development of ambulatory care haematology units in each of the five trusts within Northern Ireland is contained within Action 28 of the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-32.  The next three-year phase (25/27) of strategy implementation planning includes establishing haematological ambulatory care units and other related service developments, including stabilisation of haematology services and greater skill mix with advance nurse practitioners across Trusts. However, achieving these goals will require substantial additional resources and investment.Progressing any service developments would be subject to regional commissioning and dependent on the complexity and scale of the developments, available resource and other existing commissioning priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the effectiveness of the Job Start 50+ scheme; and (ii) to detail any steps his Department has taken to continue the programme.
answer_text: (i) Funding for the pilot allowed 180 participants to join the scheme with 160 completing their opportunity. I am pleased to advise that 83 (52%) participants moved into sustained employment, either with their JobStart employer or with an alternative employer, following their participation on the scheme.  An independent final evaluation of the JobStart 50+ pilot scheme is expected at the end of July 2025. (ii) The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the appointment of an Integrated Design Team to improve disabled access at St. Brigid's College, Foyle.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North-West rail corridor, which, if implemented, includes (i) one additional early morning service from Derry to Belfast, connecting with the 08:00 hours Enterprise; (ii) one additional early morning service from Coleraine to Derry; and (iii) service enhancements on the North-West corridor on Sundays between Derry and Coleraine, with an hourly frequency.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. In June 2024 Translink submitted a business case to: Introduce an hourly Sunday Service to Derry. Provide an earlier morning departure from Derry to connect with the 0800 hours Enterprise service to Dublin; and Extend the 22.40 service from Belfast to terminate in Derry rather than Coleraine I am currently considering this business case to examine what service improvements could be introduced to improve connectivity to the North-West. Translink is also preparing a Strategic Outline Case for the Belfast to Derry Enhancement Route Enhancement programme. This is a collection of rail infrastructure works required to be delivered in order to maximise potential and efficiencies of service on this key rail corridor. My predecessor John O’Dowd ringfenced funding in last year’s budget to secure the progression of Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine upgrades. My focus is on addressing regional imbalance and ensuring that infrastructure creates the foundations for better communities and better connections. I am confident that the projects and plans in place, will transform our social and economic geography. They will deliver on greater accessibility, improved safety and will promote balance and inclusion across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Sentencing Bill will make any changes for drug-related offences.
answer_text: Depending on the Class of drug concerned, possession of a controlled drug carries a possible maximum prison sentence of up to 7 years, while offences of production, supply and possession with intent to supply command possible maximum sentences of 14 years’ imprisonment or a life sentence.  These are significant maxima, to which I have no plans to bring forward any changes at this stage. I remain in contact with the Health Minister and our Home Office counterparts to discuss our key priorities in relation to tackling illegal drugs and the harms caused by substance abuse within our communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine rail upgrade, including a timeline for its completion.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest.The 2024/25 budget includes ring fenced funding of £4.7m, which has allowed Translink to progress this project as planned. Work has continued including the production of the detailed design of the required track improvements and preparation for the procurement phase of the project. It has also included the manufacture of railway sleepers in advance of the main works. Subject to the availability of future funding, construction work could be carried out in two phases:February – May 2027 for physical intervention work on 6 miles of track.April – June 2028 to facilitate ballast cleaning of 14 miles of track.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Irish Government to develop the Derry to Letterkenny rail line.
answer_text: An Equality Screening Exercise has been completed on the Suckler Cow Scheme and no differential impacts on Section 75 categories were identified.Farm businesses with Spring calving cows will not be excluded from payments. The Suckler Cow Scheme year runs from 1 April to 31 March. Those farmers with cows calving from January to March 2025 will be eligible for payments for year 1 of the Scheme providing their cows calve again within the first year of the Scheme and within the 415-day year 1 target. Analysis of NIFAIS data shows that 4% of suckler herds have calving events solely between January and March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the feasibility study into the reopening of the Derry to Portadown rail line.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) recommended extending the railway into Tyrone, Derry, and Donegal in order to enhance regional and rural connectivity and support direct services between Dublin, Belfast, Derry, and Letterkenny. This new corridor could also free up additional capacity on the existing BelfastColeraine/Derry route and, in the future, enable separate suburban and inter-city services on these two corridors. This feasibility study is a high-level study which has identified an initial route alignment and potential station locations for a dual track 125mph line from Portadown to Derry via Dungannon, Omagh and Strabane, which would deliver journey times that are likely to be shorter than the private car to both Belfast and Dublin. The study is also considering potential route alignments for a future spur to Letterkenny. A draft feasibility study report is expected by Spring 2025 although due to the high-level nature of the study, additional funding would be required to progress to a Strategic Outline Case.

answer_text: Officials frequently engage with Translink on provision of services including the potential amendments to scheduled services. However, the timetabling of services is primarily an operational matter for Translink.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on applications to the Back in Business scheme.
answer_text: Since the launch of the Back in Business scheme in May 2024, 31 businesses have benefitted from the 50 per cent rates discount providing almost £91,000 in rate support.The Back in Business scheme aims to help bring vibrancy, footfall and investment back to our high streets while providing businesses with a boost at the start of their business journey, helping to support jobs and bring long term vacant units back into use.The scheme offers businesses a 50% rates discount for up to two years if they move into premises which were previously used for retail purposes and have been unoccupied for 12 months or more.I would urge anyone planning to start a business or expanding their existing business to consider setting up in an empty unit and avail of the rate support on offer through Back in Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the work of the Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum; (ii) the progress made on ongoing service improvement; and (iii) the implementation at a local and regional level.
answer_text: Following the publication last year of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, the Department of Health has established a forum with Trust, GP and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level. Some of the recommendations will require investment, whilst others will be improved through modernisation and reform.Trusts have been asked to establish their own action plans to take forward recommendations and improvements which are relevant to their Trust. My officials have recently written to Trusts to ask for an update to their action plans, and they are currently reviewing progress.The regional recommendations are now in progress, led by SPPG with Trusts, Primary Care and other key stakeholders. This includes the initiation of Task and Finish Groups to develop pathways for many sub-specialty areas within Gynaecology such as Endometriosis and Menopause. Already a number of service improvements have been realised, including the development of a Red Flag Pathway, which is to be ratified by stakeholders imminently. Waiting times have also started to demonstrate improvements as services get back to full capacity, in recent months.The progress of the recommendations detailed in GIRFT will continue to be monitored by my Department in the coming months and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the application from Culmore Primary School, Derry, to transform to integrated status.
answer_text: On 10 September 2024, the Education Authority (EA) published Development Proposal 737 proposing that:-“Culmore Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status, with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter.”The statutory two-month objection period in relation to this proposal concluded on 11 November 2024.I am informed that my officials are in the final stages of preparing a report for my consideration. This has involved significant research, engagement with stakeholders, consultation on a Case for Change, objections received within a two-month statutory objection period, advice from policy teams within the Department; the professional advice of the Education and Training Inspectorate, and any other information considered pertinent to the proposal.  I am therefore unable to provide you with a precise date by which a decision will be taken. I do wish to assure you that I am aware of your desire for early clarity on this issue and I will take a decision once the report has been presented to me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to address the 513 people awaiting a Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) assessment on 31 December 2024 in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, as set out in the CAHMS Waiting Time Statistics for December 2024.
answer_text: The Western Trust (WHSCT) CAMHS continues to be challenged in meeting access targets with respect to 9-week routine (new patient) appointments. Monthly variations in the number of young people waiting for routine assessment are determined by fluctuating demand and capacity variables within the service. For instance, demand for WHSCT CAMHS services was higher in the first 2 quarters of 2024/25 financial year compared to the same period in 2023/24. 637 referrals were accepted between April-Sept 2024 compared to 571 in 2023/24. This increase of 66 was 11.5% up on previous year period.Across WHSCT CAMHS, workforce stabilisation and recruitment is the most challenging issue. In November CAMHS was operating at a 34% staffing deficit, with only 66% of actual staff in post. The service is engaged in concurrent recruitment drives in their effort to backfill into these vacant positions. The service continues to face challenges recruiting appropriately skilled and experienced staff into specialist roles. WHSCT CAMHS continue to work in partnership with Senior Trust Managers, Trust Quality Improvement and Performance colleagues with regular monitoring meetings with DOH regarding waiting times / access to the service and ensuring positive outcomes for young people in receipt of the service. Alongside vacancies, WHSCT CAMHS is experiencing an increase in Clinical acuity levels and risk laden co-morbid presentations. This requires intensive working through multi-disciplinary and multi-agency involvement/partnership in safety planning to mitigate risks. This further reduces capacity within the service as there is a need for co - working of cases. Episodes of care are generally longer and more intensive for this cohort of young people which impacts on patient flow.My Department recognises the challenges across Mental Health Services and developed the Mental Health Strategy in response. Action 32 recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed to create a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future. I am committed to working with Executive colleagues to secure all necessary resources to meet the actions outlined in the Mental Health Strategy. The Mental Health Strategy also acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to increase the capacity of (i) Health and Social Trusts' Homecare Services; and (ii) the independent sector's homecare provision to assist with hospital discharges.
answer_text: The issue of increasing capacity is linked to the recruitment and retention of staff within home care services.  Trusts operate a rolling recruitment programme for Home Care workers.  As well as this, Trusts often target recruitment in specific areas, for example in rural areas where it can often be difficult to get staff to work.  The issues noted above are similar for the Independent Sector Home Care Providers.  The increase in the regional rate to £20.01 per hour has stabilised the market, but has not significantly delivered additional capacity. My expectation is that with the introduction of the real living wage for home care workers this will attract more staff into this area of work and in the long term will assist with hospital discharges and more broadly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's current obligations on private landlords in relation to requests from those in rented accommodation to keep or acquire a pet, including response deadlines for consent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the endometriosis specialist services available in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts and Primary Care provide care for endometriosis. Management options for endometriosis include drug treatment and surgery. Most drug treatments for endometriosis work by suppressing ovarian function and are contraceptive. Surgical treatment aims to remove or destroy endometriotic lesions. The choice of treatment depends on the woman's preferences and priorities in terms of pain management and fertility. (https://bnf.nice.org.uk/treatment-summaries/endometriosis )Hormonal treatment (with a combined oral contraceptive or a progestogen) should be offered to women with suspected, confirmed or recurrent endometriosis. The patient should be informed that hormonal treatment for endometriosis can reduce pain and has no permanent negative effect on subsequent fertility. If initial hormonal treatment for endometriosis is not effective, not tolerated or is contra-indicated, the woman should be referred to a gynaecologist or specialist endometriosis service for possible further treatment, which could include other hormonal treatments or surgery. At this stage in the pathway, women referred will be under the care of a consultant gynaecologist.  The service model for endometriosis further recognises the impact of this condition on the health and well-being of women.  The basis of the model is holistic management for the needs of women undergoing diagnosis and treatment by delivering care through a specialist multidisciplinary team best able to provide responsive, appropriate care throughout the patient journey.  Each member of the team is functioning as a specialist from within their discipline whether as a consultant, nurse, or other professional lead in delivering care.Identifying the number of endometriosis specialists in place cannot be confined to a single profession, as this is not how care is provided and would be out-with best practice.  While each gynaecologist treats endometriosis, there were two accredited centres in Northern Ireland for complex endometriosis care, Belfast Trust and Western Trust.My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time.  In this regard my Department has secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time Report on gynaecology and progressing implementation of its recommendations. This will include the progression of these recommendations in the NCEPOD

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the number of people currently on the elective gynaecology waiting list for an investigative or treatment surgery for suspected endometriosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s plans to increase the availability of care home beds.
answer_text: There are 468 registered care homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within residential care homes. The greatest number of registered care homes are located within the geography of the Northern HSC Trust area. The number of registered care homes is significantly lower in Western HSC Trust, closely followed by Southern HSC Trust. HSC Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes are provided by the independent sector. Independent sector care homes account for 93% of the total care home beds registered in Northern Ireland. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023).Across the region HSC Trusts advise that there are currently no plans to increase bed provision within the Statutory Sector. They have however advised when independent sector providers develop proposals relating to the increase of care home bed capacity they engage with them and consider in line with contractual requirements and tariff. In order to explore increasing independent care home bed capacity the HSC Trusts have published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender”. These Market engagement notices explore increasing of bed stock, and have particularly focused on dementia care beds in recent years. SPPG acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. The Maximising Capacity Workstream of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any work her Department is undertaking to ensure the process of male victims reporting instances of sexual abuse or assault is appropriate and as stress free as possible.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 - AQO 1608 22-27ANSWERReporting crime is primarily a matter for PSNI who have said that all reports of sexual, domestic and child abuse are thoroughly investigated by specialist officers regardless of the gender of the victim.All victims are treated equally and investigations carried out with empathy and compassion. The same pathways exist for any victim of serious crime. Bespoke support for male victims including signposting to relevant advocacy services, is also available. Improving confidence in the system, including for male victims, is a shared priority. My Department, with the Department of Health, has launched a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy which recognises that anyone can be a victim, regardless of age, gender, sexual orientation or ability. Our aim is that Northern Ireland is a place where domestic and sexual abuse is not tolerated, and everyone feels safe and free from fear, while the Victim Charter contains key entitlements for all victims reporting crime.My Department funds Victim Support NI to deliver support to all victims of crime, including male victims of sexual offences. Services include provision of emotional support; information and advice to victims; assistance to claim Criminal Injuries Compensation; and court support services. Victim Support NI assists any victim, including male victims, to report crime to PSNI, if they wish. Sexual Offences Legal Advisers (SOLA), also managed by Victim Support NI, provide free legal advice to victims of sexual offences even prior to reporting, which may help victims to come forward. Post report, the SOLAs offer pre-trial independent advice, helping to make informed decisions and to navigate the justice system.Other measures to reduce victim trauma include new legislation giving lifetime and posthumous anonymity for victims, and the use of Remote Evidence Centres for the trial. The sharing of sensitive personal information at trial can deter victims from reporting crime, so work is ongoing to improve the court disclosure process to protect victims’ right to privacy. From April 2025, ASSIST NI will provide an adult advocacy service to victims of domestic abuse assessed by PSNI as at high-risk of serious harm, as well as victims of serious sexual offences, regardless of gender. A corresponding children’s service is planned for June, replacing the current service offered by Victim Support NI. I welcome the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime’s focus on male victims, through research into experiences of Intimate Partner Violence.

Each year due to staff turnover /staff promotions, there are opportunities to employ newly qualified OTs in the trust.
