question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the importance of trauma-informed training for those in direct contact with survivors of domestic and sexual abuse, to detail any relevant training provided to (i) judges; and (ii) legal practitioners to ensure they are adequately supporting those affected through all court processes.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of frontline professionals having an appropriate awareness and understanding of domestic abuse in all its forms, including coercive control, and how to recognise it.  This applies across the criminal justice system, as well as more widely, in terms of those who have a role to play in ensuring that offenders are brought to justice and that victims are supported.  The judiciary are entirely independent, including with respect to training, and I am bound to respect that independence. The Lady Chief Justice has statutory responsibility for the provision of training for the independent judiciary. Whilst delivery of such training is a matter for the Judicial Studies Board (JSB), which is also independent of the Department of Justice, my officials are committed to sharing with the JSB any training and awareness-raising resources that have been developed by and for our justice partners, for information where that is appropriate and helpful.In relation to legal practitioners, again, my department is not legally responsible for the regulation and training of solicitors or barristers, the responsibility for which falls to the relevant professional bodies.My Department provided the Bar of NI with access to the Domestic Abuse eLearning “Context and Change” package which was added to their CPD portal, to coincide with the introduction of the new domestic abuse offence and associated aggravators. In addition, the Bar Library organises continuing professional development training events on the subject matter and regularly advertises training events organised by the Belfast Area Domestic and Sexual Violence Abuse Partnership.A multi-agency Strategic Training Group led by my Department with membership including representatives from the PSNI, PPS and legal profession (Bar and Law Society) has been established to oversee the delivery of the recommendations relating to training as set out in the Gillen Review into serious sexual offences. To date, 30 of the 35 (86%) Gillen recommendations relating to training have been assessed as completed.This work includes the development of a new trauma-informed learning framework that sets out a range of training solutions that are intended to deliver increased knowledge and awareness and improved skillsets and sensitivity for the frontline staff dealing with victims of serious sexual offence cases in the justice system. The framework sets out training solutions available from both internal and external providers and encourages criminal justice organisations to work together to develop training solutions and where possible, promote joint training events.Work to support trauma informed practice across the public sector in Northern Ireland, specifically Health and Social Care, Education, Justice, and the Community and Voluntary Sectors, has been funded since 2021 by the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC). This initiative, led by the Safeguarding Board for NI, works to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work her Department is undertaking to address the direct correlation between increased adverse childhood experiences and likelihood of being arrested.
answer_text: In recognition of the correlation between increased adverse childhood experiences and young people coming into contact with the justice system, the Youth Justice Agency (YJA) has worked hard, with the support of the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland, to embed Trauma Informed Practice (TIP) in planning, service development and service delivery right across the Agency.    This trauma-informed approach includes the delivery of earlier stage diversion (ESD) interventions, in partnership with PSNI and the Public Prosecution Service, to divert children involved in early or lower-level offending behaviour away from the formal justice system without a criminal record and with support to help address need and desistance from further offending.More detail on this and trauma-informed practice within YJA is available in the Agency’s annual Performance Impact Report 2023-24 published in October 2024 on the Department of Justice’s web-site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on (i) the joint working group into the development of a Barnahus-style model; and (ii) the time frame for the publication of options and analysis of costings.
answer_text: The Departments of Justice and Health joint working group, which was established to develop options for a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland, is continuing to engage and to progress potential plans for consideration.  Study visits to various Barnahus examples and a benchmarking exercise to assess existing children’s services against the Barnahus standards have been helpful in scoping how a Barnahus might be taken forward in the Northern Ireland.  A recent workshop was held between working group members and operational partners to clarify possible scope and inform policy proposals.  Officials are currently establishing potential costs and are seeking to involve children and young people in their considerations, and aim to have proposals ready for Ministerial consideration for Summer 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the recent landmark study into the impact of adverse childhood experiences.
answer_text: The Prevalence and Impact of Adverse Childhood Experiences in Northern Ireland report was commissioned by the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) following observations of high trauma levels among Programme participants.  Led by Queen’s University and published on 6 February 2025, the report represents a watershed moment in understanding the profound impact of trauma on our society in Northern Ireland.  For the first time, we have robust, comprehensive data that quantifies the deep and lasting effects of both historical conflict and ongoing violence on our communities.  Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) are traumatic or stressful experiences that occur in childhood.  The report findings show that 60% of our adult population have reported at least one ACE and nearly one in five have experienced four or more ACEs.  The research also reveals a clear pattern of how trauma and disadvantage interact and compound one another. Both ACEs and conflict-related trauma are disproportionately concentrated in our most socio-economically disadvantaged communities.The correlation between higher ACE exposure and poorer life outcomes – from educational achievement to chronic health conditions – points to a pressing need for enhanced investment in targeted intervention and support.  The implications of this research extend far beyond any single department or agency.  There is an evident need to continue to develop and embed trauma-informed approaches throughout our public services and across government.  This means training workforces, redesigning systems, and ensuring that every point of contact with public services is an opportunity for support.  Addressing this level of societal trauma requires sustained, long-term investment and thought needs to be given to how resources are allocated, particularly in areas where the impact of both historical and ongoing trauma is most acute.  The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime is committed to continuing to work across government to develop a comprehensive response that matches the scale of need identified in this research and to identifying and sharing what works.  This includes reviewing existing policies and strategies through a trauma-informed lens, developing new initiatives specifically targeted at breaking cycles of intergenerational trauma, and ensuring that our investment decisions reflect the heightened needs of our most impacted communities.    In short, the report provides both evidence and impetus for change throughout our public services.  I would encourage anyone interested in this issue to take time to read the full report which is available on EPPOC’s website at www.endingtheharm.com.

answer_text: My Department recognises the important and invaluable work carried out by Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) to identify and help address community safety and confidence in policing issues, with the Department of Justice providing funding to PCSPs since they were established under the Justice Act (NI) 2011. The annual funding allocation for PCSPs has been protected for a number of years, despite competing pressures within my Department, which conveys the importance of the work undertaken by PCSPs. To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the minor works project to deliver five specialist provision classes and improve disabled access at St. Brigid’s College, Foyle.
answer_text: The business case for the delivery of a five-class specialist provision extension, with associated ancillary space, at St Brigid’s College is currently progressing through the approvals process.  This estimated £4.2m extension will greatly benefit the school and its pupils. Whilst I do not have a delivery timeline at this stage, as this is subject to not only approval of the business case, but receipt of the relevant statutory approvals which are outside of the direct control of my Department, I can assure you that this project is a top priority and all action within the control of my Department and the Education Authority is being expedited. The business case with costs of circa £830k to improve disabled access throughout the school was approved in October 2024.  The project is currently out to tender to appoint an integrated design team.  Officials have been liaising with the school and to minimise disruption it has been agreed that the works will be taken forward in phases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the financial support his Department provides to help support Young Enterprise to deliver educational programmes for pupils and students.

Educational programmes for school-age children are primarily a matter for the Department of Education. However, through Invest NI, my Department has committed £12,000 to Young Enterprise NI as part of an annual Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Business Backer Partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the financial support his Department provides to help support Young Enterprise to deliver educational programmes for pupils and students.
answer_text: My Department provided funding to Young Enterprise Northern Ireland over many years to deliver its enterprise education programmes in our schools.  Unfortunately, due to the challenging budget position in 2023-24, funding to the organisation ceased on 31 May 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the financial support his Department provides to help support Young Enterprise to deliver educational programmes for pupils and students.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of how the greenway network in Derry city has positively impacted traffic flows within the city.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data which would permit an assessment of any impact on the traffic flow in Derry as a result of the greenway network.However, the established greenway and active travel network in Derry provides people with the choice to walk, wheel or cycle as part of their everyday journey, providing an attractive alternative to those who want to avoid traffic congestion. This approach and vision is very much aligned with my Department’s objective to promote and create Cleaner and Greener connections by achieving measurable increases in sustainable travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any impact that the Living With Water Programme will have in Foyle.
answer_text: My Department published the draft Living With Water in Derry Plan for consultation on Monday 13 January 2025 and the consultation will run for 12 weeks. The geographical area covered by the draft Plan, is predominantly within the Foyle constituency.This strategic drainage Plan aims to help protect against flooding, enhance the environment and enable economic growth in the Derry area; with a focus on developing and delivering the most appropriate and sustainable solutions, by working with partners across the city.Key stakeholders including Derry City & Strabane District Council and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive have helped inform development of the draft Plan. This public consultation gives stakeholders the opportunity to comment on the drainage and wastewater issues identified to date; offer information on any additional issues they face; and express their views on the Living With Water approach to solving them.Proposed solutions include cleaner and greener infrastructure, such as Sustainable Drainage Systems and Natural Flood Management.I encourage all interested parties to contribute to this consultation.To support the Living With Water approach, my Department has also submitted a bid to the Transformation Fund to pilot the use of nature-based drainage solutions in urban areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the current electrical safety requirements for alternative housing including caravans, houseboats, tiny homes, and other non-traditional housing.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for legislating for electrical safety in the Private Rented Sector. I recently introduced Regulations to ensure that any private residential tenancy with a hardwired electrical installation in place must meet certain minimum requirements.  This applies to private residential as defined in Article 3 of the Private Tenancies Order (NI) 2006 ( The Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006). The minimum standards introduced by the Electrical Safety Standards for Private Tenancies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 stipulate that there should be five yearly inspections of a private rented property’s hard wired electrical installation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to provide consumer information to ensure the electrical safety of those living in alternative housing.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to provide further consumer information outside what has already been issued in the associated guidance to landlords and tenants under ‘The Electrical Safety Standards for Private Tenancies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024’. This guidance applies to private residential as defined in Article 3 of the Private Tenancies Order (NI) 2006 (The Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department intends to regulate installers working in non-traditional housing and ensure quality and parity with approved installer registration schemes.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to legislate on such matters. Electrical installers in Northern Ireland are regulated by professional organisations/bodies that assess and register electrical contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to ensure that those permanently living in alternative housing, such as caravans, houseboats, tiny homes, non-traditional housing, are as safe from electricity in their homes as people living in traditional properties.
answer_text: As detailed in AWQ 20332/22-27, I have already legislated for electrical safety in the Private Rented Sector.  This applies to any private residential dwelling as defined in Article 3 of the Private Tenancies Order (NI) 2006 (The Private Tenancies (Northern Ireland) Order 2006).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support available for low-income families that have been impacted by shortfalls in the Local Housing Allowance, to assist them in meeting shortfalls between housing costs paid through benefit and contractual rents.
answer_text: Discretionary Housing Payments (DHPs) provide additional temporary financial assistance with housing costs (rent not rates) for tenants in the Private Rented Sector (PRS) who may face a shortfall in meeting the cost of their contractual rent because some sort of legislative restriction has been applied. This is normally because the level of Local Housing Allowance (LHA) is below the level of contracted rent due. A small number of tenants in the social rented sector can also apply. The scheme as its name intimates is discretionary and is intended to provide temporary assistance to allow tenants some time to find a long-term, affordable and sustainable housing solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation scheme, to detail (i) the proposed duration of tenancies; (ii) how rents will be determined; and (iii) any additional floating supports that will be provided.
answer_text: A provider can specify an appropriate timeframe for a tenant to consider moving on to other forms of accommodation, however one of the conditions of the FTC loan is that there can be no predetermined end date  placed on tenancies. The programme is designed to provide safe, secure, long-term accommodation. As part of this provision any charity providing accommodation will also provide an appropriate level of tenancy support for their client base. Rent will be determined by the charity managing the accommodation but cannot exceed 80% of local market rate. Further information on the loan can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/loan-acquire-move-accommodation-lama.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how the £6.7 million for homeless prevention services is proposed to be targeted and delivered; and (ii) whether this funding increase will enable the roll-out of services such as Complex Lives in other regions.
answer_text: The Housing Executive will be utilising £3.7m of the funding allocated to homelessness through the October Monitoring Round to continue key community prevention and support initiatives. These activities are delivered within communities by third party providers, many of which provide services across Northern Ireland. The remaining £3m of funding will support inescapable salary and administrative costs related to support Housing Executive service delivery across a range of activities (including homelessness) within Northern Ireland.This additional funding will not enable the roll out of Complex Lives to other regions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Local Housing Allowance freeze for 2025 and the impact on private renters; and (ii) whether he will lobby the British Government to make the case for an unfreeze and for an increase beyond the current cheapest 30th percentile baseline.
answer_text: Rents are generally a household’s largest single expense. The increase in Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rate from April 2024 therefore assisted many renters with affordability of rent costs and with cost-of-living challenges more generally. Indeed, some households saw an increase in housing support of over 40 percent, which illustrates the scale of the impact that the 2020 freeze had on those living in the private rented sector. With the LHA rates for 2025/26 being frozen at the 2024/25 rates, the burden may once again be felt by low-income households who could face a growing shortfall between housing support and housing costs. I wrote to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions last year requesting that, going forward, LHA rates maintain pace with increases in private sector rents. I continue to advocate for LHA rates to keep track of the market rents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Housing Executive in relation to his Department's Housing Plan; and (ii) how this aligns with the Housing Executive's three-year draft Corporate Strategy which is currently out for consultation.
answer_text: My Department engages with the NIHE on a regular basis to discuss housing related matters, including the Housing Supply Strategy. The NIHE was also represented on the Housing Supply Strategy Project Board which has provided oversight throughout the development process.The Executive Housing Supply Strategy and NIHE Corporate strategy are both aligned to deliver the priorities in the Programme for Government including the provision of more social, affordable and sustainable housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of victims targeted by illegal money lending in the last two years; and (ii) any action her Department is taking to prevent such crimes.
answer_text: My Department is unable to provide statistics on the number of victims targeted by illegal money lending in the last two years.  The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) remain committed to breaking the negative influence of illegal money lenders on local communities and will continue to investigate the organised criminals involved. Unfortunately, illegal money lending is an under-reported crime in Northern Ireland.  It is a crime that targets vulnerable people when they are at their most desperate, and fear and intimidation often prevent victims from reporting it (or, indeed from getting the support they need).  This, inevitably, presents challenges for PSNI in investigating.  I would encourage anyone who has been a victim to contact either the PSNI directly or confidentially via the Crimestoppers charity at www.crimestoppers-uk.org. My Department plays a key role in the delivery of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) and remains committed to raising public awareness regarding the issue of illegal money lending.  Further detail on the EPPOC ‘Ending the Harm’ campaign is available in my response to AQW 19703/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department is taking to address illegal and harmful money lending during the Christmas period.
answer_text: My Department will continue to send a strong message that the harm caused by criminals to people and communities within Northern Ireland will not be tolerated.  Through my Department’s involvement in the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), we remain committed to providing support to those affected by paramilitarism; this includes raising public awareness regarding the harm caused by illegal money lending.The EPPOC-funded “Ending the Harm” campaign includes a dedicated advice website at www.endingtheharm.com which provides information and links to organisations that can provide support on a range of issues, including debt, for example.  A specific aspect of the campaign, highlighting how paramilitary groups use illegal money lending to control and coerce vulnerable people, was launched in June 2021; this is currently being re-run over the Christmas period. It highlights how victims, who are often struggling to make ends meet, are then exploited and preyed on for other criminal means.  Further detail on the EPPOC campaign is available in my response to AQW 19703/22-27.  In addition, the Crimestoppers Predatory Money Lending Campaign is also currently running in Northern Ireland, with the support of the PSNI, EPPOC and Advice NI, in an effort to reach out to communities to encourage people to speak up and report this scourge on society.  Anyone who finds themselves in a debt spiral and/or feels threatened, should not hesitate in reporting the situation, either to the PSNI directly or confidentially via the Crimestoppers charity at www.crimestoppers-uk.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's awareness of the campaign launched in Derry by a number of community organisations, including the Foyle Network Foundation, entitled Responsible Lending, Responsible Spending.
answer_text: My Department and the Northern Ireland Policing Board jointly fund Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) throughout Northern Ireland to address local community safety issues, with PCSPs having previously supported campaigns around harmful lending. Whilst my Department is not directly involved, I am aware of and welcome that the Derry City and Strabane District PCSP staff team were involved in the harmful lending campaign entitled “Responsible Lending, Responsible Spending”. The PCSP has advised that the Christmas billboard campaign was launched by the Mayor of Derry City and Strabane District Council on Tuesday 19 November 2024.Officers from the PCSP team attended initial meetings with the Mayor including representatives from the following organisations: Foyle Network Foundation, Community Restorative Justice, Pennyburn Credit Union, Derry Credit Union, Skeoge Community Hub, ASK Advice Services and an Independent Member from the PCSP. There is ongoing discussion with the Mayor of Derry City and Strabane District Council on a launch event to be held in February 2025, which both the Council and the PCSP intend to publicise on their social media.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is aware of any paramilitary activity targeting vulnerable people who access food banks, specifically in illegal money lending.
answer_text: Through its involvement in the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), my Department is very aware of how paramilitary-backed illegal money lenders will target vulnerable people, including those who are struggling with the cost of living. Illegal money lending is an under-reported crime in Northern Ireland, but one that we know causes significant harm within communities. EPPOC comprises a range of projects and approaches that provide support to those most at risk of being victim to paramilitary coercion. The Programme’s ‘Ending the Harm’ campaign also continues to highlight a number of often-hidden harms resulting from paramilitary coercive control, including shining a light on the specific issue of illegal moneylending. This aspect of the campaign first launched in 2021, telling the story of a young mother who felt that she had no other option but to turn to an illegal money lender when she was struggling to make ends meet. You can view the original TV ad here. This advertisement is currently being re-run over the 2024 Christmas period; it aims, not only to help to raise awareness of this particular form of paramilitary exploitation, but also to highlight that support is available.  The coercion and intimidation that comes with illegal moneylending means that it is common for victims of this crime to feel that they have no-one to turn to; the campaign is helping to ensure that those affected know that support is available and where to find it. I would encourage victims to visit the campaign’s website - www.endingtheharm.com; this signposts victims to a variety of organisations offering support and help. Advice NI, for example, is an independent Advice Network which runs a free and confidential debt service offering practical advice and support to people dealing with debt of any description. There are also many other support services locally that can help with the consequences of intimidation.   It is also important that, where possible, people report information about illegal lenders (or any other paramilitary activity) in their community to the PSNI on 101; Crimestoppers on 0800 555 111 or online at www.crimestoppers-uk.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of the Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024; and (ii) whether he plans to introduce any similar measures.
answer_text: The Leasehold and Freehold Reform Act 2024 seeks to introduce broad changes to how long leaseholds of houses and flats are managed in England & Wales. Land law in this jurisdiction differs significantly and some of the reforms – prohibiting the grant of long residential leases, extension of leasehold, and the terms of long leases – are either already in place here or are not relevant.However, I note that the Act also seeks to ensure that landlords and management companies are held to regulated standards in respect of service charges and estate management. This aspect of the legislation resonates with issues faced by local leaseholders. I have received details of several concerns about the oversight of property management companies and service charge costs and, in response to these, I have asked officials to commence work on scoping policy options. I have asked them to report to me by Easter after which I will consider how to make progress. Consideration of the 2024 Act will form part of that work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to his announcement on funding to increase short breaks capacity and expand support in the community, to outline any consultations that have been undertaken to determine the services most needed for families of children with disabilities.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's plans to increase investment in (i) Neighbourhood Renewal programmes; (ii) Areas at Risk programmes; and (iii) Small Pockets of Deprivation programmes in 2025-26.
answer_text: The Finance Minister issued an Executive Paper for Ministerial consideration, outlining Departments’ proposed Draft Budget Allocations for 2025-26. The proposals were agreed at an Executive Meeting on 19 December.Work will commence in early January 2025 to align budget allocations with Departmental priorities. The Department of Finance will now publish the draft proposals for a 12-week public consultation period and intend to publish a final budget before the start of the 2025-26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the Neighbourhood Renewal capital funding provided by his Department to each Neighbourhood Renewal area in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will extend the current mitigation package, including the benefit cap mitigation, before 31 March 2025.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my written statement issued on 5 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any additional road maintenance projects to be carried out in the Foyle constituency as a result of the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The additional funding allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round includes almost £19m towards essential and structural maintenance of our roads and street lighting work to progress existing and new schemes.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available. In addition, I have also funded a £1m targeted intervention for gully emptying given the potential impact of severe weather and resultant risk of flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the 2025-2026 budget process.

To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the recently-published research by the Chartered Institute of Housing which calls for the introduction of a new letting agent regulatory framework, including mandating the registration of letting agents and establishing an enforcement mechanism to ensure compliance with the regulations.
answer_text: My Department commissioned and funded the Chartered Institute of Housing to carry out this research. I note the findings and recommendations outlined in the research report.However, having taken account of the limited time available in the current mandate and the pressing issues currently facing the private rented sector, I will not be able to progress letting agent regulation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the renewed Housing Supply Strategy, to detail any actions his Department has considered in relation to youth homelessness and addressing the increased risk of homelessness facing care-experienced young people.
answer_text: Whilst one of the five key objectives of the Housing Supply Strategy is the prevention of homelessness and prioritising housing solutions for those most in need, the prevention of Youth Homelessness is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  The Housing Executive has recently published a Youth Homelessness Action Plan which supports the delivery of the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27. Young people who have been part of the care system will be central to the Youth Homeless Action Plan which was developed in partnership with a range of organisations in the statutory and voluntary sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Supply Strategy will be published before the end of November 2024.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy was published on the 5th December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA 943 as endorsed by his Department in February 2024.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail progress made by his Department to bring forward an agreed long-term model of indemnity for GPs.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland.  A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model of provision to a fully state-backed indemnity scheme. I have given a commitment for this work on options to be completed by the end of this calendar year, to allow for substantive engagement with GP representatives on the topic as part of negotiations for the 2025/26 GMS contract.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the review of Together: Building a United Community.
To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Renters' Voice survey on no fault evictions, published in October 2024.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether any additional financial support will be allocated to homeless service providers from the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: An additional Non-Ring-Fenced Resource allocation of £11.3m has been made available as part of October Monitoring.  My Department is currently working through the process to determine allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department's Residential Building Safety team will explore possible avenues of redress for homeowners affected by defective blocks, such as potential VAT exemptions.
answer_text: There is no evidence to suggest that defective blocks affected by mica or pyrite is a systemic issue across Northern Ireland, therefore such an exploration is not justified at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to (i) homeowners affected by defective blocks.; and (ii) homeowners suspected to be affected by defective blocks.
answer_text: My officials have liaised with Derry and Strabane to understand the extent and potential impact of defective blocks in the local area and beyond.  My department has corresponded with one homeowner known to be affected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with HMRC in relation to possible VAT exemptions for homeowners affected by defective blocks.
answer_text: My Department has had no engagement with HMRC about this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progressing the secondary legislation that will establish a framework for controlled reservoirs under the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing five pieces of secondary legislation that will further commence and implement the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015. At its meeting on 17 October 2024, the NI Executive agreed to the progressing through the Assembly, of the five pieces of secondary legislation that will establish a reservoir safety framework under the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015. Officials are now progressing this secondary legislation through the Committee for Infrastructure, with a view of enacting it as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of a grant scheme to assist reservoir managers to meet their obligations under the Reservoirs Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing business cases for financial assistance for those that need it, both for initial supervision and inspection costs, and then capital works. In a press release on 17 October 2024, I reassured reservoir owners that I intend to introduce a grant scheme for those who need it, assisting reservoir managers to meet their obligations under the Act.  This will be subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 113/22-27,  to outline what progress has been made to address waiting times for urgent orthopaedic surgery in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I remain committed to reducing orthopaedic waiting times across the region.  The demand for orthopaedic services continues to exceed current funded capacity across both assessments and treatments. This capacity demand gap has been exacerbated by the growing elderly population and the increased incidence of trauma fractures has put considerable pressure on the elective services. While the Western Trust continues to improve the utilisation of their orthopaedic theatres, additional capacity is required to address the current levels of demand.£76m investment has been approved non-recurrently this year for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments across a number of specialties including orthopaedics. The Western Trust has already secured Independent Sector orthopaedic insourcing to South West Acute Hospital and further orthopaedic capacity has been secured from other Independent Sector providersThe current orthopaedic waiting times are unacceptable. Earlier this year a bid was made by my Department for £135m to help reduce elective waiting lists, including orthopaedics. Unfortunately this bid was not supported, which was a missed opportunity to improve our waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 113/22-27, what further consideration has been given by his Department to include the reduction of waiting lists in orthopaedics across all Health and Social Care Trusts as part of the broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.
answer_text: Addressing waiting times continues to be a key priority for me.  Strenuous efforts are being made across the Health and Social Care service to reduce the current long waits.  In the current financial year £76m has been allocated to HSC Trusts to maintain red flag and time critical treatment to ensure those with cancer or other time critical conditions (life, limb or sight threatening) can receive the care they require. My Department bid for an additional £135m of non-recurrent funding for this financial year to address the growing backlog and directly support up to 70,000 additional patients with appointments and treatments. Specifically for orthopaedics, this included a specific bid of £53 million to address some 4,400 patients waiting over 2 years for orthopaedic procedures. In addition I sought £31m for the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme which would have allowed treatment for up to 4,000 patients, many who would be waiting for orthopaedic treatment.  Regrettably, none of that bid was met in the 2024/25 Budget. The funding allocated to my Department in June monitoring was insufficient to enable me to cover the cost of delivering existing services. As a result, I was unable to allocate any additional funding to address the Waiting List position.  However, my Department have continued to seek additional funding to be provided to assist with addressing waiting lists. A £10m bid has been submitted as part of October Monitoring which unfortunately was unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14699/22-27, how many children benefitted from the planned waiting list initiative to be provided in October.
answer_text: The waiting list initiative in October was only one part of a number of actions that were commenced by the Trust in attempts to increase the numbers of children who could avail of this procedure.A total of 4 children had their procedure undertaken as part of the waiting list initiative. A further 8 children had their procedures undertaken in scheduled lists. The Trust will continue to identify possible means to reduce the numbers of children on this list but it must be noted that significant challenges remain and will continue to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his recent visit to the Amp Growth Incubator.
answer_text: Question 10 – AQO 1118/22-27I met with the team at The AMP to discuss growth in the North-West and how supporting innovative start-ups can help boost the local economy.The AMP is one of the delivery partners of Founder Labs, Invest NI’s accelerator programme, which provides technology-based start-ups with mentoring, support and access to finance, to help them scale globally. Ten start-ups from across the North will complete the first programme in December.  Impacts to date include increased sales in export markets and private investment secured. This will help drive productivity and support my plan for a regionally balance economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 14787/22-27, and given the recent coverage of legal aid, when her Department will share the report of the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services.
answer_text: Further to my response to AQW 14787/22-27, my officials are currently in the final stages of preparing to present the foundational report into civil legal aid, alongside that of the review of criminal legal aid, and the work relating to taxation reform, for my consideration.I am expecting to receive these shortly and I will share this with the Assembly once I have fully considered the findings and proposals for reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to the 17 recommendations set out in the September 2024 Blood Cancer UK report, Taking Blood Cancer Out Of The Shadows: A Plan To Increase Survival In The UK.
answer_text: Whilst my Department acknowledges the helpful 17 recommendations outlined within the UK Blood Cancer Action Plan, it should be stated that it was not consulted or involved in the development of this Action Plan. However, the recommendations around the themes of workforce, diagnosis, care, treatment, and access to clinical trials and data, are all areas we acknowledge require work, and we have been working to address these via the NI Cancer Strategy 2022 to 2032. Blood cancer /haemato-oncology services in NI have been under growing pressures for some time amidst serious workforce concerns for haematological services UK wide.  In order to address the severe regional pressures within haematology services, in 2023 my NI Cancer Policy and Projects Team initiated a project for the redesign of Haematology Treatment Pathways across NI to include the three main haematological pathways: Myeloma, Lymphoma and Leukaemia. Three reports with recommendations were completed in March 2024.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of my Department were asked to implement the recommended actions of these reports in April 2024. SPPG have engaged with Trusts on the reports’ recommendations.  Some progress has been made on early stage actions including, baseline assessment and identification of nursing workforce gaps, maximising virtual clinics, identifying appropriate time and process for holistic needs assessment, weekly multi-disciplinary Team (MDT) meetings, adoption of NICE guidance and development of a haematology cancer nurse career pathway. Many actions across all 3 reports are dependent upon reform and greater skill mix within haematology services. While some recommendations are cost neutral, others require investment, and resource to implement.  Progression of some key actions is dependent on consultant recruitment and retention as well as additional investment in greater skill mix for the nursing workforce to include more Advanced Nurse Practitioners.  This will help create capacity to take forward a number of the recommended actions.   Action 16 of NI Cancer Strategy 2022 - 2032 is to develop a specialist integrated haematological diagnostics service (SIHMDS) for Northern Ireland. This will provide more timely, co-ordinated and equitable diagnosis for people presenting with blood cancers and will be taken forward when funding becomes available.Clinical trial uptake is low in Northern Ireland and this is acknowledged in the NI cancer strategy under Actions 55 and 56. Work will be undertaken to address these actions over the lifetime of the NI Cancer Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the funding allocated to each of the 29 Family Support Hubs in each of the last two years.
answer_text: A Family Support Hub is a network of statutory, voluntary and community sector organisations that provide locality based early intervention Family support services. There are 29 Family Support Hubs across the 5 Health and Social Care Trust areas in Northern Ireland. Hubs do not directly provide services to families. They are a signposting and referral service which promote and coordinate a collaborative interagency response to support families whose needs do not meet the threshold for statutory social services. There are variations in commissioning approaches across the 5 HSC Trusts in respect of the use of funding for the coordination of hubs. Three of the HSC Trusts allocate funding for the coordination of their hubs through a range of Community or Voluntary sector organisations identified in the tables below as Lead organisations (Western, Belfast and Southern HSC Trusts)One Trust allocates funding for hub coordination to one Voluntary sector lead organisation (Northern HSC Trust)The family support hubs in the South Eastern HSC Trust area are managed by a Trust employed Coordinator who forwards hub referrals   to 3 voluntary sector providers who have Service Level Agreements with the Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the criteria used by the Department to set the (i) Housing Association Guide; and (ii) Total Cost Indicator rates for Housing Associations.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for issuing guidance to Housing Associations via the Housing Association Guide which provides advice to Housing Associations around aspects of Development, Finance, Procurement, Governance, and Housing Management functions. A review of the guide is underway to ensure the guide is up to date, fit for purpose and user friendly so it meets the needs of both Department and Housing Associations. 		The Total Cost Indicator (TCI) system is used to determine the cost to deliver social housing. Department of Finance’s Construction and Procurement Delivery staff (Housing Advisory Unit) use a range of information to establish a benchmark of total average costs for acquiring and constructing social homes across the 11 Council areas. The methodology for calculating the TCI uses land costs provided by Land and Property Services, construction costs taken from the Project Information Forms supplied by Housing Associations and On-Costs which are reviewed from the costs supplied by the Associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress made on completing the design stage of the Drumahoe Flood Alleviation Scheme.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified at this stage and consultants have been instructed to progress the detailed design phase of this project. It is intended that the detailed design phase will be complete by early 2026. Following this, progress to procurement and construction will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress his Department has made with the developer, South Bank Square Ltd, since 20 September 2024 in relation to progressing the signalisation of Caw roundabout, Derry.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of Caw roundabout is included in the planning conditions for the adjacent H30 housing development and it is the responsibility of the Developer to deliver these works.My officials have completed a review of the information provided by the developer on the 20 September and met with the developer on 23 October to discuss their proposals, and the timing for delivery of their works to Caw Roundabout.My department’s focus is to ensure that the proposals are safe, that they mitigate the effect of the increased traffic caused by the new development, that they do not prejudice other adjacent road users and that progress continues towards delivery in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans for the delivery of the next phase of the North West Development Fund.
answer_text: The current phase of the North West Development Fund runs to the end of March 2025 and is fully committed.  The Fund is a joint initiative with the Irish Government, so any decisions on a future or second phase will require engagement and agreement with them. We are aware of the Irish Government’s commitment in principle to further funding, in collaboration with ourselves, which was made in New Decade, New Approach. We also recognise that stakeholders in the area are keen for a further phase of the Fund.Officials, along with Irish Government colleagues, have engaged with Derry City and Strabane District Council and Donegal County Council, through the North West Strategic Growth Partnership, to discuss the aims and objectives for a potential second phase of the Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of resident's parking schemes.
To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on legislative plans to improve the rights of renters; and (ii) whether it will address the issue of pet ownership by renters.
answer_text: My Department is progressing a programme of Private Rented Sector (PRS) reform.  A key component of this work is achieving a balance between the rights and responsibilities of landlords and tenants.   Recent changes to private tenancies law that have increased the rights and protections for tenants include:Requirements for certain information to be provided on commencing and during a tenancy;A limit on deposits that can be requested and held;The requirement for receipts for cash payments; and Measures to increase fire safety.Ongoing work that will further enhance these rights and protections, includes the development of regulations on electrical safety and to allow the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods.  It is anticipated that both these regulations will be in place in the coming months.		I am currently considering what further actions I can take over the reminder of the mandate that will further improve the safety, security and standards of the sector.		I currently have no plans to address the issue of pet ownership by renters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department is undertaking with the Western Health and Social Care Trust to address waiting times at the Accident and Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether there is any regulation or legislation in relation to companies that manage ground rents and charge homeowners a communal or service fee for maintaining green space within housing estates.
answer_text: Property management companies are subject to general company law legislation as set out in the Companies Act 2006. However, there are no additional regulatory or legislative requirements. It is an issue upon which I am keen to see further policy development undertaken during this mandate and I am exploring with officials how best progress can be made in the context of available expertise and competing legislative priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the draft Programme for Government commitment to deliver an ambitious programme to decarbonise all housing stock, to detail his Departments plans to contribute to achieving these targets.
answer_text: I am working closely with Executive colleagues to deliver the draft Programme for Government commitment on decarbonising housing stock, which will involve a range of initiatives including, improving energy efficiency and installing other decarbonisation measures to comply with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and address fuel poverty issues.My Department leads on the Residential Buildings Sector of the NI Climate Action Plan, which will set out the residential sector policies and proposals to decarbonise housing to meet the first carbon budget for 2023-2027. My officials are also working closely with other Departments and stakeholders to develop a long-term residential decarbonisation strategy, to achieve Net Zero by 2050. Initiatives include a Housing Supply Strategy, with an emphasis on improving energy efficiency and decarbonised solutions, a new Fuel Poverty Strategy and a replacement for the Affordable Warmth Scheme. Discussions are on-going with DfE on an energy efficiency policy, low carbon heat policy and developing a One Stop Shop, to provide advice and signposting for citizens to appropriate grant schemes. Much of this work will require further policy development and public consultation.My Department’s continued action in this area will improve energy efficiency, reduce carbon emissions, lower heating bills, improve health and well-being and support green growth jobs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration his Department has given to the non-regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: Management companies must act in accordance with existing relevant companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department.However, the issue of how shared property is managed also has an interface with substantive land law and is one that I am keen to see some progress in developing policy proposals. I am currently exploring with officials how to secure the appropriate expertise in what is a complex area. Further progress will depend on that and competing legislative priorities over the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on a waiting list for occupational therapist appointments, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) length of time on the waiting list.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the (i) Review of the Disabled Facilities grant; and (ii) Patient and Client Council survey to gather feedback on the Disabled Facilities Grant process.
answer_text: (i) The Review of the Disabled Facilities Grant is part of a wider review of (NIHE administered) Private Sector Grants (statutory and discretionary) being taken forward by my Department in conjunction with the Housing Executive and Department of Health. This is an in-depth, holistic review of the operation of both mandatory and discretionary grants. The Terms of Reference of the Review have been agreed and a cross-Departmental Project Board has been established. Work is currently progressing on a range of different workstreams as described in the Terms of Reference. (ii) The Department of Health asked the Patient and Client Council to conduct a survey of people who have interacted with the Disabled Facilities Grant process.  On 26 September, feedback was presented to the Project Board, who are currently considering the survey findings to inform the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the demand for (i) minor; and (ii) major home adaptations for older people and people with a disability to remain in their homes.
answer_text: There is continued demand for both minor and major housing adaptations for older people and people with a disability across tenures. My Department is committed to ensuring that adapted and accessible homes are available and suitable for those who need them and that budgets are maximised for the provision of these homes. I have contacted the Northern Ireland Housing Executive who have confirmed that:During financial year 23/24, the Housing Executive commenced a total of 3,805 minor adaptations for its tenants. This included 954 adaptations for those with a disability.  There were also 119 referrals across all regions for major adaptations during the 2023/34 financial year.The Housing Executive also administer the Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG), which provides funding for adaptations to private sector homes to enable older people and those with a disability to remain in their homes. The Housing Executive have confirmed that: For the period 1 April, 2023, to 31 March, 2024; the Department received 2,199 Occupational Therapist recommendations for Disabled Facilities Grants in relation to older people and those with disabilities. This includes both minor and major adaptations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of nurses that are trained to operate fibroscan machines, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are a range of staff disciplines involved in operating the fibroscan machines available in each Trust as follows:Belfast Trust – the Trust has nine non-consultant grade staff trained to operate the two fibroscan machines. This includes 3 co-ordinators, 3 nursing staff, 1 healthcare assistant and 2 medical staff. Northern Trust – the Trust has trained one nurse to operate the machine.South Eastern Trust – there are currently 2 nurses trained to operate the Trust fibroscan machine.Southern Trust –the machine is based within radiology and it is the radiology staff who will be using this technology in the first instance.Western Trust – the fibroscan machine in the Trust is currently operated by a specialty doctor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the level of investment available for disabled facility grants for the (i) 2023-24; and (ii) 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the closing budget allocation in relation to the Disabled Facilities Grant for the 2023-24 financial year was £12.465m and that the indicative budget allocation for the 2024-25 financial year is £13.149m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have received a fibroscan in the last twelve months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following points summarise the activity position reported by HSC Trusts.:Belfast Trust – in the 12 months from 1st June 2023 and 1st June 2024 there were a total of 1,429 scans undertaken by the hepatology service in the Trust. Northern Trust – in the 12 months from September 2023 to August 2024 313 scans were carried out in the TrustSouth Eastern Trust – there are approximately 35 fibroscans delivered each month in the Trust. This would equate to around 420 scans undertaken in the last 12 months.Southern Trust – the Trust has advised that they have a more modern advanced machine – Canon i800. This was procured with a ‘Shear-Wave Elastropraphy’ (SWE) package which is a similar concept. The use of the SWE package is limited and the service is not fully operational. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of fibroscan machines operating in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: As at September 2024, Belfast Trust had two fibroscan machines operating as part of the Hepatology Service in the Trust. The other four Trusts have one machine each.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any action his Department and the Housing Executive is taking to raise awareness of the Housing Executive’s Financial Inclusion Team; and (ii) whether the Housing Executive has any plans to further expand its Financial Inclusion Team in future years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive routinely raises awareness of the Landlord Services Financial Inclusion Team via multiple methods, predominantly to tenants. Making Your Money Work means that tenants are advised of and referred to the Financial Inclusion Team via patch and income collection staff in the normal course of rent accounting and tenancy management duties. This is incorporated into the Making Your Money Work service which includes providing a benefit and budget calculation, referrals to third parties for debt advice or floating support and highlighting the work of the Financial Inclusion Team. New tenants will be referred to the Financial Inclusion Team as necessary; the current referral triggers are new tenants, tenants with rent arrears, tenants at risk of legal action and tenants who have engaged with an external debt advice provider.Further to the above mandated trigger points for a financial inclusion intervention, frontline staff will also outline the Financial Inclusion Team’s work to customers who have particularly complex financial circumstances (e.g. disability/health status, foreign nationals, student, or self-employed), or when the tenant is asking multiple benefit related questions which may not be easily remedied by frontline staff. The service is also advertised to tenants in receipt of a housing cost benefit with dependent children in their property, tenants who have lost a housing cost benefit entitlement, tenants who have seen a reduction in their housing cost benefit entitlement, and those who have had a significant change in their circumstances which affects their financial wellbeing.The work of the Financial Inclusion Team is also a key component of the Housing Executive’s response to the Universal Credit Managed Migration (Move to UC) programme. Tenants who have made a claim for UC as part of Move to UC, and who are identified as needing advice and assistance, are referred to the Deputy Financial Inclusion Managers. All ESA(IR) cases are also routinely referred to the Financial Inclusion Team for advice and assistance to ensure their claim is migrated without disruption. The work of the team is discussed with the tenant before the referral takes place. This work has been invaluable for tenants who are nervous or unsure about the Move to UC.Alongside their normal day-today duties, the Financial Inclusion Team attend various events to raise awareness of the ways they can provide advice and assistance to Housing Executive tenants. The expansion of the Financial Inclusion Team in 2023 has meant they are able to spend more time in the community, providing in-person advice and assistance. As a result, the Financial Inclusion team have established five separate, regular drop-in clinics and throughout 2023/24 they attended 23 other one-off events with more planned for the 202

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025.
answer_text: The Racial Equality Strategy provides a 10-year framework for action over the period 2015 to the end of 2025.Of the eleven proposed actions in the Strategy, the majority have been completed, with the remaining actions intended to continue throughout the lifetime of the Strategy.Whilst it will remain a key priority to fully deliver the commitments in the current Racial Equality Strategy, it is important that post 2025, there is a clear mechanism and overarching framework for achieving racial equality in our society. An initial workshop took place on 11 September 2024 with the Racial Equality Subgroup and Travellers and Roma Thematic Groups to gather initial thoughts on the way forward. Feedback from this workshop, along with input gathered through a process of collaboration with key stakeholders and a public call for views planned for early in the new year, will help inform any future strategic approach. The violence and unrest experienced this year demonstrates now more than ever the importance of the strategy and its future replacement in building a more cohesive society for all.  It will also reaffirm the Executive’s commitment to continuing to change our society for the better.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the involvement (i) tenants; and (ii) voluntary and community organisations, including housing associations, will have in the Housing Executive’s strategic review of the rent scheme.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Housing Executive provided me with the following information:“The Review of the Rent Scheme is currently within the scoping and research phase. Once this has been completed the project team will engage with stakeholders through various forums such as the Central Housing Forum and the Housing Community Network to inform the work of the review and to take on feedback. Additionally, as per statutory requirements, formal tenant/public consultation will be carried out providing information on any proposals for change and allowing time for tenants, as well as other stakeholders, to provide their response and feedback. It should be noted that housing associations each operate their own method or model for assessing the weekly charge for their properties. The NIHE Rent Assessment scheme only applies to a very small number of ‘controlled’ Housing Association properties where the tenancy commenced prior to September 1992.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the timeline for the completion of the Housing Executive’s strategic review of the rent scheme.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided me with the following update: “The Review of the Rent Scheme is within the initiation stage and the scoping and research phase of the project has commenced. A Project Board has been constituted and project documentation has been drafted for approval by the Project Board. Any proposals regarding the Rent Scheme will be subject to statutory requirements such as approval from the Department for Communities.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the foundational review of civil legal services.
answer_text: My officials are currently finalising a report on the findings of the foundational review of civil legal services that I expect to receive shortly.I will use that report, the review of criminal legal aid, and work to prepare for the reform of legal aid taxation to inform decisions about the future direction of legal aid reform, which I will share with the Assembly and with stakeholders at the appropriate time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his Department's plans to review the current skills and training landscape for young people with special educational needs after they leave school, in order to provide recommendations for improvement.
answer_text: In June, I asked my officials to commence a review of the current skills and training provision for young people with special educational needs and to provide me with recommendations for improvements. I also asked my officials to examine legislative protections and bring back advice as soon as possible.  The work is complex and involves many different stakeholders and issues so it will take time, but the project team is now in place, funding for the work has been allocated from the Skills Fund, and the planning and scoping activity is underway. The team has taken early opportunities to hear the views of a range of stakeholders by participating in a series of DE-led parent/carer stakeholder sessions, meeting with other Departments and FE Colleges, engaging with organisations such as Clanrye, Mencap and the Equality Commission and offering to brief the All-Party Group on Learning Disability.  In addition, my officials have developed a bid to the Transformation Fund for support in relation to support and provision for school leavers with special educational needs, which has successfully progressed to stage 2 of the assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to improve school facilities at St Brigid’s College, Derry.
answer_text: St Brigid's College was one of twenty-eight schools announced in March 2022 to advance in design as a major works project. In April 2023, all of the twenty-eight projects were put on hold, due to the capital budget position. Seven of these projects were subsequently announced to re-start in February 2024, however at this time St Brigid's College remains on hold.A business case for the provision of five Special Educational Need (SEN) classrooms, together with associated resource space, is currently being reviewed by my Department’s economists. Child specific disability access works are also planned at the school and the business case for these works is currently being developed by the Education Authority. The relocation of a bus shelter for SEN pupils is due to complete within the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the People and Place review, including when it will be out for consultation.
answer_text: The Review of the Executive’s People and Place strategy has progressed well with collaborative structures in place to facilitate engagement with a wide range of key stakeholders and other focus groups from across Northern Ireland.This work aims to produce a sound evidence-based approach that could be used to identify areas experiencing deprivation and on which a future funding programme to tackle deprivation could be based.  The Development of an outline Strategic Framework, informed by the views expressed through the stakeholder and sectoral engagement exercise has broadly concluded with a successful Strategic Insight Lab event in June.Subject to my consideration and that of my Executive colleagues, the Strategic Framework will be subject to a full public consultation exercise. Following a public consultation exercise, the planning assumption is that the new Strategic Framework will be completed during 2025, followed by a transition phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the new fuel poverty strategy, including when it will be out for public consultation.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 6599/22–27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the extended schools investment for the Foyle constituency in 2024-25, broken down by (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: £8.1m in Extended Schools funding was recently confirmed for the 2024/25 academic year. A total of £748,675 has been allocated to 35 Primary and Post Primary schools in the Foyle constituency. Twenty-seven primary schools received an average of £18,975 in funding. Eight post-primary schools received an average of £29,544 in funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there is a National Data Strategy or National Data Guardian to improve access to government data to better inform research and policy.
answer_text: The Open Data Strategy published in October 2019 and covering the period 2020-23 is currently under review to assess its impact to help inform a future strategy. An Open Data Portal is in place to facilitate easy access to public sector data for both reuse and redistribution. The Open Data Portal hosts 864 datasets, published by 84 organisations and there have been over 40,000 downloads of data hosted on the portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children, broken down by (i) length of wait; and (ii) constituency.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 93 children currently on the waiting list for this elective procedure. All of these children are on BHSCT lists as the procedure is carried out at the Royal Belfast for Sick Children (RBHSC). Due to the introduction of the new ICT system, Encompass, in BHSCT it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage. It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined.My officials within SPPG have now carried out some scoping on the further question that you have raised with senior officials in BHSCT and have been informed of the following plans to help address this issue.A Paediatric Surgeon is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable to transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.Belfast Trust is hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in October on a Friday, with patients being nursed in Knox ward in RBHSC over the weekend. This will be dependent on theatre and bed capacity in RBHSC.A further initiative is also being considered were a number of patients could receive their surgery in the Ulster Hospital by Belfast consultants.  Belfast Gastrostomy Nurses will provide training to staff in SET to help facilitate this. There is also currently a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery.The Trust are also examining partnership working with the Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital. This will be dependent on theatre and bed capacity in RBHSC.In the meantime, paediatricians and paediatric surgeons along with other clinical teams at the RBHSC will continue to support these children and their families.My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

To ask the Minister for Communities when the Housing Supply Strategy will be published.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to place the role of the Victims of Crime Commissioner Designate on a statutory footing.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any intention to repeal the Vagrancy Act 1824 by the end of the current Assembly mandate.
