question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 464/22-27, and in light of his Department's agreed budgetary position, what discussions his Department has had in relation to funding allocations from 30 June 2024 onwards for neighbourhood renewal projects.
answer_text: On 5 June I announced the Department’s initial Budget 2024-25 allocations and officials are currently working to ensure that all groups receive their grant funding agreement for the 2024/25 financial year by 30 June 2024.  I have made the decision to maintain the level of Resource funding provided to Neighbourhood Renewal projects despite the significant shortfall in both Resource and Capital budgets.In addition, for those Voluntary and Community Groups (including Neighbourhood Renewal groups) receiving funding support from Community Empowerment Division (CED)  and Voluntary & Community Division (VCD) I have made an additional £1.45M of Resource funding available in 2024-25.  This will equate to an additional 5% of funding for all Neighbourhood Renewal organisations and will form part of their funding agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of research from Crisis that shows people who are rough-sleeping in England and Wales are 17 times more likely to have been victims of violence, (i) what research is being done in relation to the level of rough-sleeping; and (ii) what plans his Department has to prioritise the prevention of homelessness.
answer_text: This issue of what research is being done in relation to the level of rough-sleeping is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has worked with the Welcome Organisation, Depaul and First Housing Aid and Support Service to carry out a Street Needs Audit in Belfast, Derry/Londonderry and Newry. One of the main objectives of the Street Needs Audit was to undertake research on individuals engaged in rough sleeping and street activity throughout the day and night. The Street Needs Audit report is due to be published in the coming weeks.” The Housing Executive Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 and accompanying Year 2 Action Plan set out the need to make a strategic shift to prevention. It is my intention that homelessness should be rare, brief, and non-recurrent. However, it will take time to achieve these ambitions and my short-term focus will continue to be supporting those currently in crisis. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Ulster University Magee Taskforce.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10967/22-27, (i) for an update on the findings of the research report; and (ii) whether he will legislate to ban illegal letting fees before the end of this Assembly mandate.
answer_text: As previously advised, my Department is due to receive the research report at the end of June. The Department will then deliberate on the findings of the report before considering policy options for letting agent regulation here, including the potential banning of fees.	Implementation of banning illegal letting fees will require primary legislation, which will be considered due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of ongoing reforms to notice to quit periods and the increase in homelessness in recent years, whether his Department plans to legislate to amend Section 3 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988 to extend the number of statutory days applicable to homeless prevention duties.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“During 2023/24, 9 Housing Need Tests were completed at the following settlements:LGDSettlementAntrim & NewtownabbeyMoneyglassCauseway Coast & GlensBurnfootLislagan Derry & StrabaneTamnaherinMid UlsterBallinderryMoortownArdboeGulladuffNewry, Mourne & DownSpaThe test at Moneyglass returned a healthy response which will enable NIHE to support a small housing development in the settlement. A site is currently under investigation by Rural Housing Association. Following the test at Burnfoot, NIHE have plans to develop a social housing scheme of up to 14 homes.The test at Tamnaherin has resulted in a new build scheme being added to the Social Housing Development Programme, which will deliver 6 new social homes by Apex Housing. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps being taken to address the lack of available social housing supply for larger families, particularly those with additional mobility needs owing to disability.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The delivery of  new social housing units for larger families and people with disabilities will be delivered through the SHDP 2024/25 – 2026/27, subject to confirmation of the annual SHDP Budget. Housing Executive Place Shaping teams will continue to liaise closely with housing associations to ensure that housing mixes being agreed for new social housing schemes reflect the requirements of larger families and people with disabilities. The Housing Executive also provides funding to housing associations to carry out adaptations to existing housing association stock to meet the requirements of people with disabilities. The Housing Executive are working with the Department for Communities, Department of Health, HSC Trust occupational therapy service and service user forums to improve pathways and housing options for people with a range of disabilities including mobility impairment. Actions include the development of an Assurance Framework with the setting up of an Interdepartmental Programme Board and Partnership Forums with a focus on promoting a collaborative approach to service delivery between health and housing. Such collaboration helps to ensure a consistency of approach and better-informed future planning for disability based on need”.The construction of new social homes will be dependent on the available budget. The Executive has recently agreed the 2024/25 budget, I will now decide how this is allocated across my department. Funding for the for the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for 2024/25 has yet to be finalised.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6566/22-27, whether he has included an increase in salaries using National Joint Council pay scales for groups in receipt of funding from his Department in his bid to the Department of Finance.
answer_text: My Department took account of the forecasted requirements, including salary costs, for groups it funds, as part of bids submitted in the 2024-25 Budget exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce legislation to govern the charging of letting fees, to enable greater transparency from letting agents and ensure adequate penalties and enforcement arrangements are in place.
answer_text: My Department has commissioned research to aid consideration on potential letting agent regulation, including the banning of fees. The research report is scheduled to be provided to my Department by the end of June.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will receive the Co-Design Group's report and proposed action plan on the Irish Language Strategy.
answer_text: The drafting of an action plan for the Irish Language Strategy by the Co-Design Group was completed in November 2023. Its contents are now being considered by a Cross-Departmental Working Group of senior officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7117/22-27, (i) for an update on the independent research which was due to complete by the end of March 2024; and (ii) what developments have been made in respect of the outstanding Fundamental Review of Allocations Proposals 7 and 9.
answer_text: As you are aware, implementation of eighteen of twenty proposals in the Fundamental Review of Allocations are underway. The remaining two proposals (7 and 9) are subject to further exploration and therefore longer-term delivery. NIHE estimate that these will be delivered, in Stage Four (estimated mid-end 2025). As part of this exploration, independent research into Proposal 7, was commissioned by NIHE and is currently being finalised. Officials will work with the NIHE to develop suitable options for my consideration. Further research on Proposal 9 is planned by the NIHE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeline on the proposed legislation for delivery of the extension to notice to quit periods.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022 stipulates that the extensions to notice to quit periods can only come in effect when the Department has introduced regulations setting out exceptions.  This was a late addition to the Act and no prior policy work had been carried out on the concept.Work to date has revealed the complexity of the issues involved.  Key considerations include equality issues, such as, in cases of domestic violence or when anti-social behaviour is as a result of a mental health condition. At present my officials are producing a robust Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA). I anticipate that both the EQIA and draft regulations will be ready for consultation in late Summer and, subject to a positive response, will be in place by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Homeless Connect CRISPP Survey Results presentation; and (ii) whether additional monies will be allocated from the Department to support Supporting People organisations to provide the minimum wage increase.
answer_text: AQW 10830/22-27Ciara Ferguson MLA has askedTo ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Homeless Connect CRISPP Survey Results presentation; and (ii) whether additional monies will be allocated from the Department to support Supporting People organisations to provide the minimum wage increase.AnswerIn relation to part (i) of your question I have yet to receive Homeless Connect’s CRISPP Survey Results; however, I have sought the requested information from the Northern Ireland Housing Executive Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive acknowledges the findings of the CRISPP commissioned survey of Supporting People (SP) funded independent providers with responses from 23 providers which found that the majority of respondents were concerned about the increase in the minimum wage.  The Housing Executive continue to actively engage in discussions with the Department for Communities, in seeking to address financial challenges.’’ With regards to part (ii) of your question I can confirm that The Executive has recently agreed the 2024/25 budget and I will now decide how this will be allocated across my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) whether a similar approach to the Complex Lives project, being undertaken in Belfast, would be useful in Derry and the North West; and (ii) what needs to happen for this to take place.
answer_text: Complex Lives is a collaboration model that enables a range of providers to deliver services jointly. The Housing Executive have advised that any expansion would “have to be based on need, stakeholder willingness and commitment to collaborate both strategically and operationally, to undertake work on the necessary system changes to allow for joint commissioning, information sharing and to secure long-term funding arrangements.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department will promote community wealth building.
answer_text: Work was undertaken in 2021/22 by my officials to consider how the principles of community wealth building were being advanced and how they could be further embedded in policy and delivery plans in my department.  Additionally, investment was made in capacity building, through increasing awareness and understanding in local government and in the community sector. As we deliver on our plans across Housing, Regeneration, Employability and Disability, as well as refreshing our policies on Place Based Anti-Poverty and wider Community Support, I will ensure that we are linking social and economic policy outcomes, improving inclusion and maximising social value in all that we do.   For example, I intend to make an announcement in the coming weeks in relation to enabling greater access to social finance for community sector and social enterprise organisations to support sustainability and growth, including the creation of new jobs for those who can often find themselves locked out of the labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what legislative competence his Department has to improve regulation of management service agreements in respect of apartment complexes.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer given to AQW 10454/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he is aware of the lack of regulation relating to management service agents and agreements in respect of apartment complexes.
answer_text: There is currently no regulatory body responsible for oversight of the functioning of residential developments with management companies, nor is there a regulator responsible for managing agents. My Department has an interest in this issue in relation to the substantive law relating to leasehold property.While the majority of such arrangements appear to work well, I am aware of a number of specific complaints against the conduct of such companies and agents, and around issues such as transparency in respect of service charges. This is a complex matter requiring significant expertise and future work will be dependent on competing priorities and available resources, and I will engage with officials to assess how these issues can be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he intends to legislate to strengthen regulations and improve transparency in respect of management service fees.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer given to AQW 10454/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the (i) scoping exercises; (ii) research; and (iii) stakeholder engagement carried out by hi Department's Residential Building Safety team in relation to (a) a new building safety act; and (b) changes to existing legislation in respect of residential building safety standards.
answer_text: The Residential Building Safey Division has commenced scoping exercises and research and introductory engagement sessions have been held for residents, industry and the public sector.  Work is currently ongoing to formulate policy which will support new legislation and changes to existing legislation.  Whilst it is not yet certain, it is anticipated a new Building Safety Act is likely to be required for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's consideration of removing the barriers to housing access for people in the private rented sector, particularly in relation to estate agent requests for homeowner guarantors for all applicants, regardless of their income and employment status.
answer_text: Large up-front costs including deposits are a barrier for many people accessing the private rented sector.  To help address this issue, my Department introduced legislation to restrict landlords from asking for or holding a deposit for more than one month’s rent. This legislation was operational from 01 April 2023 and therefore covers tenancy agreements made on or after this date. Of course, deposits are not the only cost for those accessing and living in the PRS.  At present, some letting agents charge a wide range of fees to both prospective and existing tenants, including credit check fees, application fees and other administrative fees. My Department has commissioned research to aid considerations on potential letting agent regulation including banning of fees, as a way of further reducing the  affordability barrier.  The research report is scheduled to be provided to my Department by the end of June. My Department has no responsibility for guarantors nor does private tenancies legislation stipulate the need for guarantors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the system in the private rental sector that requires applicants to have a guarantor who is a homeowner; and (ii) how this disproportionately impacts lower income families and those coming from overseas, which contributes to inequality in the private rental sector.
answer_text: Private tenancies legislation does not stipulate a requirement for guarantors, whether or not they are homeowners.  Some individuals may find it more difficult to find a guarantor. I am aware of organisations that can provide the services of a guarantor, for example, for students. I am also aware that some landlords and agents accept alternative forms of security, such as rent in advance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what consideration she has given to introducing legislation to protect retail workers from assault.
answer_text: I condemn all abuse and violence directed at any frontline workers. No-one should face mistreatment whilst doing their job and serving the public and such attacks must not be tolerated. Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. However, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a frontline worker in my proposed Sentencing Bill and will give further consideration to protections for these workers in that context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether consideration will be given to increase the maximum allocation of £12,000 for successful applications for roof and window repairs to thatched properties within the Historic Environment Fund.
answer_text: Yes. Though applications for support in the current financial year have closed and are capped at £12k, the Historic Environment Fund scheme for next year is currently in development. Should this proceed, subject to available budget, it will consider that construction costs, including those relating to thatching, have increased over recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any visits are planned to listed thatch properties, particularly those which remain lived-in properties, to assess the ongoing challenging facing thatched homeowners including vermin damage, poor ridging and leaking rain water.
answer_text: I have no plans to visit any thatched properties at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration he has given to reviewing the Department for Infrastructure industrial workers AA pay grade and bonus scheme.
answer_text: A full formal grading review of all industrial workers within the NICS was undertaken in 2016.  This graded all Roadworkers, Storespersons and some River Workers as Industrial 1 (AA analogous).Any review of grading would be the responsibility of Department of Finance and would only be appropriate if significant changes had occurred to the work activities of the staff since 2016.My officials are currently engaging with DoF and industrial trade unions in a process to investigate options for the future of the Roadworker productivity bonus scheme. A working group has now been established and Terms of Reference are currently being agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he will give to reviewing support for owner occupiers who are unable to maintain or upgrade their World War II prefabricated homes.
answer_text: At present I have no plans to review support in relation to World War II prefabricated homes. The  Housing Executive do, however, offer the following discretionary grants:Repair grant.Renovation grantReplacement grantHome repair assistance grantThese may be appropriate depending on the circumstances of the owner occupiers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures his Department is considering to support listed thatch property owners, particularly in respect of implementing rules for specification, ridging and depth of thatch.
answer_text: My Department provides financial support, for the repair of roofs of listed thatched buildings.  All supported work must comply with published grant advisory standards. Following engagement with owners over the last year, more detailed guidance is in development to improve owner awareness and knowledge of thatching. This will include guidance on specification ridging and depth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on upgrading street lights in the Derry City and Strabane District Council area to LED street lighting.
answer_text: To date, 70% of the lanterns within Derry City & Strabane District Council Area have been upgraded to LED units, it is anticipated that, subject to availability of funding, the remaining lanterns will be upgraded within the 2024/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of how regional transport plans change how people commute.
To ask the Minister of Education what capital investment is planned for the statutory Youth Service in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: I am making the case for additional and sustained capital investment in our education infrastructure and have submitted significant bids to include capital investment in the youth sector.  It is anticipated that a budget for 2024-25 will be agreed by the Executive and presented to the Assembly by April/May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the business case for a new build youth centre in Skeoge, Derry.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed it does not have a Business Case for provision of a youth centre in Skeoge. The EA has advised, that moving forward, the provision of youth services in the area is to be delivered by the Community and Voluntary Sector, who will be supported by Derry City & Strabane District Council to facilitate the provision of the capital build.  A potential site for the youth centre is on land belonging to the EA and ongoing discussions are being facilitated by the Council in regard to this site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has any (i) current; and (ii) future plans to update the NI Multiple Deprivation Measures.
answer_text: The Multiple Deprivation Measures plays a crucial role in assessing levels of deprivation and ensuring that finite resources are directed to those most in need. In that context, I am keen to revisit the Deprivation Measures to ensure that decision makers have access to the most appropriate and up to date data. NISRA are currently exploring options to update the NIMDM to account for new data including the 2021 Census, new statistical geographies and how to resource an update factoring in wider budget pressures. Specific timescales are not currently available, however NISRA will keep key stakeholders and users updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to progressing the Education Authority Pay and Grading Review for education support service workers.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 9211/22-27.This answer will be contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 29 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what support is being provided to registered childminders to meet minimum standards.
answer_text: The 2012 Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 were developed by my Department to promote the delivery of a consistent level of service quality and safety, and also to facilitate a consistent regional approach to registration and inspection. The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 requires Health and Social Care Trusts to register childcare providers and inspect them on an annual basis. The Standards were written in this context and are intended to support the aims of the legislative framework. There are 16 Minimum Standards grouped under 4 headings: Quality of Care; Quality of Staffing, Management and Leadership; Quality of the Physical Environment; and Quality of Monitoring and Evaluation. Through the provision of funding, the Department offers a wide range of core and continuous professional development training courses free of charge to childcare providers. In 2023/24 49 of these courses were accessed by over 600 childminders.The Department also holds annual contracts with the Northern Ireland Child Minding Association (NICMA), intended to sustain and increase childminding provision across Northern Ireland. Under these contracts NICMA, among other things:Prepares childminders for inspection and facilitates the development of individual quality improvement plans in association with the Trust Early Years Teams as and when required following inspection visits;Enhances childminders’ knowledge and understanding in relation to supporting children’s development; Delivers training and development opportunities directly to childminders and signposts to other core and continuous professional development opportunities; andFacilitates access to childminding peer support groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of dental practices that provide health service dentistry, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: There were 364 General Dental Practices across Northern Ireland at 31st December 2023, the latest publicly available quarterly Accredited Official Statistics. The number of General Dental Practices broken down by Assembly Area is detailed in Table 1 overleaf. Information is only available for those dental practices and dentists who have made arrangements with the SPPG (previously HSCB) to provide health service dentistry. Information is not held in relation to dental practices that are wholly private.Table 1: Number of General Dental Practices by Assembly Area at 31st December 2023Assembly AreaDental Practices1,2Belfast East20Belfast North27Belfast South33Belfast West19East Antrim16East Londonderry17Fermanagh and South Tyrone24Foyle20Lagan Valley19Mid Ulster15Newry and Armagh29North Antrim26North Down19South Antrim11South Down20Strangford13Upper Bann20West Tyrone16Northern Ireland364Source: Family Practitioner Services Dental Payment System, Business Services Organisation1 Active dental surgeries at the 31st December 2023; an active surgery is one which can be linked to having had at least 1 active dentist, who has made an arrangement with the SPPG (previously the Health and Social Care Board) to provide health service dentistry, registered to that surgery i.e. the dentist’s start date was before the time period in question and finish date was after this point or currently still open.2 Dental Practices includes a number of Orthodontic and Specialist Practices. Orthodontics is a specialist area of dentistry concerned with the growth and development of the teeth and jaws and the prevention and treatment of abnormalities of this development. Therefore, most patients are children. These practices do not typically have registered patient lists, with instead patients normally seen on referral from a GDS dental practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to address the level of NHS dentistry services in Foyle.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it had not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  On 27 March I announced a 6% pay uplift for dentists for 2023/24, in line with the recommendations from the DDRB (Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body). The uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I also announced an additional £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay.£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25. This will support public access to priority treatments.£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme;I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that were not registered with a dentist, referred to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group and secured emergency dental treatment in the last 12 months, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients not registered with a dentist, who were referred to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and subsequently secured emergency dental treatment, is not available.Patients contact SPPG for a range of queries including accessing emergency dental treatment.  A record is kept of patient contacts at each local office along with a short summary of the query.  If a patient is unregistered and requires emergency dental care, the SPPG provide these patients with the names of the practices participating in the Pilot for Unregistered Patients, Asylum Seekers and Refugees (PUPAS).  SPPG have no record of whether these patients make contact with these practices and the outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to reviewing the current General Dental Service contract.
answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however the pandemic, followed by the absence of an Executive has hampered progress in this area.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK in recent months, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting a similar approach in Northern Ireland.In addition, officials have held a number of engagement events with practitioners from across NI, the results of which will help inform decision making on the future of General Dental Services.I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that General Dental Services is sustainable for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the delivery of the Strategic Framework to Ending Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: We remain committed to bringing forward the Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls as soon as possible. Preparatory work is already underway, and our officials are currently finalising a draft two-year Implementation Plan, with a focus on what can be delivered in the first year. The draft Framework and accompanying Action Plan is expected to be submitted for Executive consideration and approval in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what consideration she has given to setting up a process to monitor compliance with the Victim Charter.
answer_text: It is vital that everyone working across the criminal justice system upholds the rights and entitlements laid out in the Victim Charter, and it is for each organisation to embed the importance of compliance with the Charter within their own workforce.  Monitoring compliance with the Charter is also one of the objectives of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime. I recognise that, across the criminal justice system, organisations need to be able to demonstrate compliance with the Charter and this is an area where more focus is needed.  That is why, in collaboration with the Commissioner’s Office, my Department is planning to establish a multi-agency Victim Data Working Group.  One of the group’s priorities will be to explore how compliance with Charter obligations can be better monitored and demonstrated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions are being taken to ensure adequate funding is available to address the range of challenges facing housing supply.
answer_text: The Executive is still to agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known, I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s budget against key priorities, including housing.In terms of addressing the challenges facing housing supply, my Department has developed a draft housing supply strategy. The strategy has been developed using a whole system approach, which recognises there is a wide range of interconnected issues that impact on supply. These issues cut across several Executive Departments and therefore addressing these challenges will require a collective agreement and significant investment. I am currently reviewing the strategy with the aim of bringing a finalised version to the Executive for its endorsement and commitment to support its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to introducing a targeted lung cancer screening programme.
To ask the Minister of Justice what funding her Department has provided to the seven probation-approved premises, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: Approved Premises act as a transition between prison and resettlement back into the community for high-risk offenders leaving custody. All residents are supervised by Probation Board NI with a proportion subject to monitoring by PSNI and under Public Protection Arrangements NI. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the difficulties which can occur over the weekend in terms of accessing suitable housing and support services, whether she will consider introducing similar legislation to the Offenders (Day of Release from Detention) Act 2023.
answer_text: My Department understands the problems that can arise when an individual is released from custody over the weekend, and the challenges this presents in accessing vital support services, including to accommodation.The Prison Service takes a proactive approach in preventing homelessness by ensuring that everyone coming into custody has their housing needs assessed, and those exiting back into the community have accommodation on release or are signposted to available options. To support this ‘The protocol for the management of the accommodation and related support needs of people in custody in Northern Ireland’ was published in December 2023, which sets out a collaborative approach to addressing accommodation needs from NIHE, PBNI and NIPS. To support the implementation of the protocol we will also be introducing NIHE Housing Advisers into prisons in April 2024 to support the accommodation advocacy role currently undertaken by our VCS partners, Housing Rights.Cognisant of the challenges of weekend releases, the Lady Chief Justice has helpfully agreed to move her High Court Bail listings from a Friday commencing 8th April 2024.With a particularly congested legislative programme across all of the Executive Departments, it will not be possible for me to bring forward any additional Assembly Bills beyond the three that I am committed to delivering to modernise aspects of the justice system; to reform sentencing arrangements following a review of sentencing policy; and to tackle hate crime.  However I have asked officials to monitor ongoing legislative developments in England and Wales, including whether their introduction has had a material impact on reoffending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what funding her Department has provided to the Complex Lives project in each of the last three financial years, including 2023-24; and (ii) what funding her Department intends to provide to the project in 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department values the work of the Complex Lives project and the support they provide to those at risk of chronic homelessness, many of whom are in contact with the criminal justice system. Due to wider funding pressures my Department has only been able to provide limited support over the last three financial years, with a total contribution of £40,000 made to the organisation in this period.As my Departments’ budget for 2024/25 has yet to be confirmed I am not in a position to consider future funding of the Complex Lives project at this time.It is important to recognise that no single organisation can tackle chronic homelessness alone.  It is essential there is a cross-Departmental approach to addressing the underlying factors which can contribute to homelessness, including poor health outcomes, such as substance misuse and mental health challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what funding her Department is providing for the Short Sentence Support Programme; and (ii) to detail the agreed objectives of the programme.
answer_text: An emerging theme from recent reports by the Criminal Justice Inspectorate for NI and the NI Audit Office has been the need to better support prisoners serving short sentences. In response, NIPS has partnered with a VCS organisation to deliver a bespoke programme, titled the Short Sentence Transition Programme, to manage the transition of short-term prisoners into the community by targeting the factors which positively impact on their desistance from reoffending.We envisage that this pilot programme, which commenced in Maghaberry Prison in January 2024, will run for 2 years and will be the subject of a full evaluation at that stage.  NIPS contribution to the programme to date has been £27,568 for 2023/24.  A decision on the level of funding for 2024/25 will be taken when the budget for the new financial year has been agreed and allocated.  Objectives of the programme include: To better assist short-term prisoner’s smooth transition to the community and resettlement in the early period post-release;Engage in an offence free lifestyle through implementation of learning with the ‘Let’s Talk’ Programme which will be completed prior to release from custody;Access fundamental needs e.g. benefits, G.P. registration, banking and obtaining I.D;Develop a sense of hope for the future; Identify appropriate pathways and support to training, education and employment, based on the needs of the service user; andOn release from custody support service users with engagement into specialist services for addiction and mental health to manage and improve these identified areas of need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an assessment of the level of youth homelessness; and (II) what departmental plans there are to support young people to secure appropriate accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation and has provided the following information. In respect of supporting young people to secure appropriate accommodation, the Housing Executive has developed a Youth Homelessness Action Plan.The Youth Homelessness Action Plan has been developed to reflect the development of legislation over recent years with much of this legislation recognising the collaboration that is required from organisations such as the Housing Executive and Health and Social Care Trusts in particular, alongside a wide range of organisations in the statutory and voluntary sectors. The legislative context that has guided the development of this Action Plan highlights the need for collaborative working to minimise the risk associated with children and young people falling into any gaps as they transition between services.The Youth Homelessness Action Plan aims to provide a coordinated response to ensure the Housing Executive addresses the challenges posed by youth homelessness with a focus on prevention, support and accommodation options. Addressing homelessness and housing need amongst young people and families with children is a distinct priority for the Housing Executive, and as an organisation it is wholly committed to achieving better outcomes for these cohorts.  Subject to approval, the Youth Homelessness Action Plan will be published in April 2024.The table below details the level of youth homelessness and details presentations and acceptances for single persons aged between 16 and 25 years old over the past five years:PRESENTERS Single MalesSingle Females16-17 years18-25 yearsTotal16-17 years18-25 yearsTotal2018/19661,4291,495891,2521,3412019/20681,3221,390901,1951,2852020/21501,4571,507841,2851,3692021/22461,2331,279501,1331,1832022/23391,0421,081421,0631,105ACCEPTANCES Single MalesSingle Females16-17 years18-25 yearsTotal16-17years18-25yearsTotal2018/1933655688538288812019/2032666698447768202020/2117594611367818172021/2227559586217067272022/231746448126697723

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of women on a waiting list for a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis diagnosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of women on a waiting list and the average waiting time for a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis diagnosis is not available centrally in the Department.  HSC Trusts were asked to provide this information, however only the Northern Trust were able to provide a response with 110 patients currently waiting and an average wait of 37 weeks.The remaining Trusts were unable to provide this information as this level of detail isn’t recorded on the hospital systems at present and would require a manual clinical review of patient records.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Electronic Travel Authorisation scheme.
answer_text: Oral Question No 14 – AQO 188/22-27I am concerned about the impact of the introduction of the British Government’s Electronic Travel Authorisation Scheme on our tourism sector. I have met with the NI Tourism Alliance to discuss this vital issue and to hear at first hand the industry’s concerns. Tourism is performing extremely well with the number of external overnight visitors quadrupling between 1998 and 2019. Seamless all-island travel is key to this growth. Over 70% of international visitors to the north arrive via Dublin.I intend to raise this matter with the Home Office to try to find a solution that protects our tourism industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his assessment of the current water and sewerage capacity in Foyle; and (ii) the impact this has on future development.
answer_text: The issues you raised are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for women on a waiting list for a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of women on a waiting list and the average waiting time for a laparoscopy as part of an endometriosis diagnosis is not available centrally in the Department.  HSC Trusts were asked to provide this information, however only the Northern Trust were able to provide a response with 110 patients currently waiting and an average wait of 37 weeks.The remaining Trusts were unable to provide this information as this level of detail isn’t recorded on the hospital systems at present and would require a manual clinical review of patient records.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of (i) road safety; and (ii) congestion at the Caw roundabout in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Caw Roundabout is one of the busiest junctions in Derry City.  There have been twenty reported collisions over a twelve-month period in 2022 that occurred either on Caw Roundabout or within the immediate approaches.  The figures for 2023 are not yet available.As a result of significant development underway in the vicinity of the roundabout, a scheme to upgrade and signalise Caw Roundabout is currently conditioned to the new development.  My officials are continuing to engage with the developer to facilitate their delivery of these upgrades.  In addition, further upgrades to improve the overall safety and performance of this junction are also being considered by my Department

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the options explored to jointly fund with the developers of the H30 Ballyoan project, for the full upgrade of the Caw roundabout in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: There are currently two options being explored by my Department and the H30 developer to upgrade Caw Roundabout.  The first option is for the developer to deliver the upgrades to the roundabout conditioned as part of the planning permission for the H30 site.The alternative option could be for a developer contribution to be agreed between the developer and the Department.  Subject to agreement, under this arrangement the cost of construction, traffic management, supervision and service relocation would be funded by the developer and the Department would then complete the design and carry out the construction works. A number of meetings have been held to explore this option and a further meeting is being scheduled with the developer in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6117/22-27, to detail the priority ranking for St Brigid's College on the Mayor Capital Works programme announced in March 2022.
answer_text: Twenty-eight schools were announced in March 2022 to advance in design under the Major Capital Works programme. St Brigid’s College was ranked eleventh in the prioritised list.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to increasing the (i) hourly rate of payment; and (ii) preparation fee for NHS interpreters.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will however be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  Nevertheless, I have already asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review (i) the hourly rate of pay and (ii) the preparation fee for interpreters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (ii) how many ophthalmologists are working in Foyle; and (iii) how many ophthalmologists are needed to cover Foyle.
answer_text: (ii) HSC workforce is not broken down by constituency, rather by Trust. There are currently 8 consultant ophthalmologists working for the Western Health and Social Trust, which includes Foyle, with the breakdown as follows: five full-time, one locum and two part-time.The Western Health and Social Care Trust are funded for 9.5 whole time equivalent ophthalmologists. Recruitment work is ongoing within the Trust to fill the gap in consultant ophthalmology posts.(iii) Workforce recruitment is undertaken to address need. In addition to the medical (ophthalmology) workforce, need is also met by deployment of multidisciplinary teams within the Trust ophthalmology service, and by integrated care pathways involving community optometrists. This strategic direction is demonstrated, e.g. by the use of optometrists, orthoptists, and advanced nurse practitioners in Trust macula, glaucoma, and other specialist clinics. It is also illustrated by the use of primary care optometrists in the Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS), glaucoma pathways, and Ocular Hypertension (OHT) monitoring in community optometric practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6115/22/27, what consideration he has given to increasing the Western Health and Social Care Trust's capacity to deliver (i) care home placements; and (ii) domiciliary care packages.
answer_text: (i) There are 468 registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing Home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within Residential Care Homes. Independent sector Care Homes account for 93% of the total Care Home beds registered in Northern Ireland. The Western Health and Social Care Trust have 31 nursing homes and 36 residential care homes with a total of 2079 care home beds. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).Western Health and Social Care Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes are provided by the independent sector. In order to explore increasing independent sector care home bed capacity the Western Health and Social Care Trust published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender” in May 2023. These Market engagement notices had an aim of increasing of bed stock, particularly dementia care beds. As a result, two independent care home providers have come forward expressing interest in increasing bed capacity. The Trust have advised should other providers within the independent sector wish to develop a proposal to increase bed capacity they would engage with them and consider the proposal in line with contractual requirements and tariff. My department acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. Workstream 1 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services. (ii) The Western Health and Social Care Trust Trust Annual Domiciliary Care budget amounts to £46.3m this includes an additional £1.353m allocated to meet demographic growth for the last two financial years. The Trust currently deploys all of the Domiciliary Care resources allocated.  This amounts to an average of 39,000 weekly hours of care provided to 3620 service users.  Despite the resources deployed the Health and Social Care Trust is not able to meet all of the presenting demand for domiciliary care services.  At present there is unmet need amounting to 652 cases assessed as requiring 5202 weekly hours.  All Domiciliary Care providers advise that they are encountering workforce challenges which is the predominant factor affecting the Trust's ability to increase domiciliary care service provision.  The Western Health and Social Care Trust has embarked on a Homecare Optimisation programme as part of Delivering Value.  The reorganisation of domiciliary care services in 5 of the Trust's 9 geographical areas has resulted in the accommodation of 459 new or increased care packages totalling 4267 week

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the level of housing need in rural communities; and (ii) what actions his Department is taking to address the shortage in social housing in rural communities.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring the vitality and viability of our rural areas and to addressing rural housing need. One of my key priorities as Minister is delivery of social housing, and I know that delivery in rural areas presents significant challenges and requires different solutions than urban areas. My Department has set up a Steering Group which includes representatives from Department for Communities, Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, NI Housing Executive, and Land & Property Services. The overall aim of the Steering Group is to explore the barriers to rural housing development and develop, agree, and oversee the implementation of a plan which will aim to address them. These barriers include availability of land, grant, need, infrastructure, and planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Supporting People Programme, what action his Department is taking to (i) ensure continued service delivery; and (ii) recruit and retain staff.
answer_text: The Supporting People Programme provides grant funding to approximately 85 delivery partners who provide around 850 housing support services for circa 19,000 service users across Northern Ireland. My Department has sustained funding levels for the Programme and on 6th March 2024 I announced an additional £3 million in funding for the Supporting People Programme.My Department and NIHE will continue to engage with the sector in these challenging times to seek ways to ensure the long-term resilience of the Supporting People programme and continued service delivery; ensuring value for money that allows the programme to keep delivering real outcomes for service users – those most vulnerable in our society.The Housing Executive is not the direct employer for Supporting People staff and therefore, from a contractual perspective, has no role in the recruitment or retention of staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to the delivery of a specialist therapeutic service for victims of sexual violence and abuse after 31 March 2024.
answer_text: On 4 March 2024 in my statement to the Assembly, I advised that funding for this specialist counselling service for victims of sexual abuse would be extended for a further 12 months.I advised that the one year extension would allow proper consideration and a decision on and implementation of the best way forward for counselling of this nature.  The ending of this contract was not a cost saving measure.  The intention has always been to use the funding to continue to support people who are victims and survivors of sexual abuse in line with the draft domestic and sexual violence strategy 2023-2030.I provided reassurance regarding my ongoing commitment to the importance of the provision of specialist counselling services for victims of sexual abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on a waiting list for ophthalmology surgery, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) constituency.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people on the (i) routine; and (ii) urgent waiting list for ophthalmology surgery.
answer_text: The tables below outline the number of patients awaiting (i) routine and (ii) urgent inpatient or day case admission within the ophthalmology specialty.It has not been possible to separate patients waiting for surgery from the ophthalmology waiting list. An unknown number of non-surgical and diagnostic procedures are included in the figures presented.Cataract surgery is the most high-demand surgical intervention in ophthalmology regionally, requiring both urgent and routine treatment.Following prioritisation in rebuilding after the pandemic, there are currently 2,694 patients on regional cataract surgical waiting lists (212 waiting over 52 weeks). For comparison, this total figure was 4,145 in March 2022, of whom 1,361 were waiting over one year.Figures presented exclude the South Eastern Trust as data relating to 31st December 2023 was not available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records following the launch of the Encompass programme.Table 1: Number of Patients Waiting Under the Ophthalmology Specialty by Urgency and Weeks Waiting - 31st December 2023UrgencyWeeks WaitingTotal0-6>6-13>13-21>21-26>26-52>52(i) Routine5007137133291,2351,5805,070(ii) Urgent8377093761202513282,621Northern Ireland1,3371,4221,0894491,4861,9087,691Source: DoH Inpatient Waiting Times Dataset

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6114/22-27, to detail the number of people that secured orthopaedic surgery under the RoIRS scheme, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In response to your enquiry the number of people that secured orthopaedic surgery under the RoIRS scheme, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is as follows –Western Health and Social Care Trust – 590Southern Health and Social Care Trust – 798South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust – 532Northern Health and Social Care Trust – 720Belfast Health and Social Care Trust - 321

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans his Department has to end the Right to Buy scheme in the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I have no plans to bring forward legislation to end the Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s House Sales Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the delivery of the five-year homelessness strategy and the demand for non-standard forms of temporary accommodation, to detail any discussions he has had in relation to acquiring additional funding to help meet the assessed need identified by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s budget requirements for temporary accommodation have been fully met this year.  The Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s allocations against key priorities.Non-Standard (including hotel and B&B) accommodation is entirely unsuitable for households, including families with children, to live in for extended periods.  I would emphasise that the use of non-standard accommodation is only used out of necessity.  This is due to an unprecedented demand for temporary accommodation arising not from an increase in Homelessness presentation or acceptance, but from a change in the pattern of usage.  This has resulted in more people availing of the Housing Executive’s offer to provide them with temporary accommodation.  I have asked the Housing Executive to provide me with a monthly update on the issue of Temporary Accommodation, including progress to address the current position. This will allow me to be fully satisfied that the Housing Executive is doing all it can to reduce the use of temporary accommodation, and the resultant cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any discussions with ministerial colleagues in respect of delivering a standalone housing outcome in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: I have not had any specific discussions with ministerial colleagues in respect of delivering a standalone housing outcome, as the process of establishing and agreeing the direction and potential format of a new Programme for Government is yet to be convened by the Executive Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on establishing a Financial Transaction Capital loan programme to acquire Move-On Accommodation.
answer_text: The business case for the Financial Transaction Capital Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation has been drafted and is currently being reviewed by Departmental officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the use of full body scanners within the prison estate.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to (i) resource; and (ii) complete the Second Survey of Listed Historic Buildings.
answer_text: The Second Survey was terminated in 2015 following budget reductions. At that point it was approximately 70% complete. The outstanding requirement included surveys of approximately 3600 unlisted buildings along with 1500 incomplete records from the “first survey”, of which approximately 700 related to buildings currently unlisted.Budget constraints mean that there are limited resources available to address this issue. A small team is in place. It is currently prioritising the 700 unlisted buildings for which incomplete records are currently held alongside queries in respect of buildings that have the potential to meet the Criteria for Listing and are under threat from demolition or adaptation (buildings most at risk). My officials continue to bid for further funding to complete the survey as opportunities arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he has given to (i) the Independent Welfare Review recommendations; and (ii) setting out an agreed timeline for reporting the outcome.
answer_text: I have noted the recommendations that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022.I can advise that officials are currently taking forward work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Advisory Panel’s Review to help inform decisions on this matter, as any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years. I will provide further details to the Assembly at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when the consultation on a new debt respite scheme will commence; and (ii) when a report will be published.
answer_text: My officials have developed draft debt respite policy proposals in preparation for a public consultation, which I expect to consider in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on progressing the Housing Executive Revitalisation programme.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 5484/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given to expanding access to education psychology services to all preschool settings.
answer_text: All Department of Education (DE) funded pre-school education settings can currently access Educational Psychology Services through a variety of pathways.  As part of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN), DE is considering the range of Education Authority (EA) support services available to all schools and educational settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on progressing the independent advice and debt policy and delivery framework.
answer_text: My officials have worked closely with the Advice sector and engaged extensively with stakeholders across Northern Ireland to develop proposals for a fresh policy framework for integrating independent community-based Advice and Debt services.  Proposals are currently being finalised together with Councils, as they co commission some of these services.I anticipate that a public consultation will be launched before the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to increasing the departmental funding available for children and adult hospices in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: I refer to my statement of 9 February 2024 in which I confirmed an increase in recurrent funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice by £420k, an amount which was previously subject to confirmation each year. This increased recurrent funding to £1.6m per year, giving the Hospice greater financial certainty from 2024/25 onwards. I also noted that the financial challenges facing the Hospice go much deeper than this and that, in the current economic context, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.Given that the Executive has not yet agreed a budget for 2024/25, and that my Department is currently forecasting a funding gap of some £1 billion across the full range of health and social care priorities, I am not in a position to consider further increases to Departmental funding for hospices at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6285/22-27, to detail a timeline for the completion of the (i) Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification and Residential Rehabilitation Services; and (iii) Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan.
answer_text: In September 2021, the Department of Health launched Northern Ireland's 10-year Substance Use Strategy with the vision that ‘People in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs; have access to high quality treatment and support services and will be empowered to maintain recovery’. To deliver the vison and outcomes of this Strategy, the Department officials were tasked with the co-production of a new Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework.Alongside the development of the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, the Department has progressed two other related early priority pieces of work; a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust Area, and,an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland.  The recommendations from both of these work streams will help inform the final Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new Strategic Plan.(i) The Population Needs-Based Assessment for Substance Use in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area was completed in Autumn 2023. This work was completed following engagement from a wide range of stakeholders across the Western Trust area, including HSC statutory Addiction Services, Mental Health Services, Community and Voluntary Sector Services, Housing, Homelessness Services and People with lived and living experience of substance use. Key recommendations from this assessment are currently being considered by the Western Drug and Alcohol Community Team (W-DACT) and will be prioritised for implementation from April 2024 onwards. The recommendations will be taken forward in line with the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan for Substance Use and aligned with Northern Ireland’s Regional Single Mental Health Service, and Integrated Care System.(ii) In 2023, the Department of Health commissioned an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland. This review has involved extensive fieldwork and engagement with service providers and service users as well as visits to providers in other jurisdictions to view alternative models of practice. The work on this review completed in February 2024 and the initial report is now being reviewed before a final agreed report is presented for Departmental approval. Once finalised, this review will be subject to a public consultation process before the recommendations are taken forward. It is anticipated that the 12-week public consultation period will commence prior to Summer 2024. (iii) The Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementati

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the current capacity at the Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) to meet demand; (ii) how many trained staff are currently working at the RFC; and (iii) how many trained staff are needed to cover the centre.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed that the Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) is currently fully staffed to deliver its current activity.On 15 February, I announced that publicly funded In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women. This increase was possible from within the RFC’s existing resources, which includes 39.35 whole time equivalent staff. While I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the RFC will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust will develop a business case for the further expansion of the service, which will include information on additional staff required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he will give to securing alternative routes into the speech and language therapy profession to help it grow, such as apprenticeships and Masters degrees.
answer_text: My Department will keep under review the potential for apprenticeships and Masters graduate entry programmes to contribute to widening and deepening access to the Speech and Language Therapy profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he will give to support the introduction of a professional speech and language therapy advisor to sit across his Department and the Department of Education, to embed Speech, Language and Communication Needs at a strategic and policy level across departments.
answer_text: My Chief Allied Health Professions Officer will examine the option of a Cross Departmental Speech and Language Therapy advisor to ensure speech, language and communication needs are considered at a strategic and policy level across healthcare and education settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he has given to (i) commencing policy development to replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme; and (ii) setting out an agreed timeline for the policy development work.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty intervention that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026.  This new scheme will align with the Climate Change Act, the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy, including the need to ensure a ’just transition’ to lower carbon homes going forward.My officials are currently planning pre-consultation stakeholder engagement events to inform the design of the new scheme.  This series of workshops and focus groups will seek the views of those impacted by fuel poverty and those with a strong interest and influence on fuel poverty policy and delivery.Following this stakeholder engagement, the draft policy, regulations, and delivery framework to support the new scheme are anticipated by March 2025, with a public consultation to follow in the succeeding quarter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he will give to increasing the number of speech and language therapy undergraduate places at Ulster University for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: I commend the significant contribution made by Speech and Language Therapists to the delivery of care, and to service transformation across the HSC. The commissioning of pre-registration education and training by my Department is reviewed annually, taking into account a range of factors including staffing and vacancy levels, recommendations from workforce reviews, priorities, and affordability.  A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places on this and other programmes at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce. However, I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the level of investment in community transport in the last three years; and (ii) the proposed level of investment for 2024-25.
answer_text: The table below provides the level of revenue funding invested in community transport in the last three years.2021/222022/232023/24Rural Community Transport to support Dial-a-Lift services £2,236,081£2,236,080£2,030,291Rural Community Transport Covid Relief Funding to support Dial-a-Lift services£438,841Urban Community Transport to support the Disability Action Transport Scheme£2,125,942£2,125,942£2,055,077TOTAL£4,800,864£4,362,022£4,085,368I am not in a position to commit to funding in 2024/25 in the absence of an agreed budget. I will be engaging with my Executive colleagues on a budget for the incoming financial year and will be seeking support for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider providing greater access to family mediation outside of the legal system, similar to that in neighbouring jurisdictions.
answer_text: Mediation can bring many benefits, particularly in private family law cases. Provision is already available through funding provided by the Department of Health but I am committed to working with Ministerial colleagues who share responsibility for mediation to explore how we can enhance its use. I hope to initiate a pilot to explore family mediation further as part of the Private Family Law Early Resolution Action Plan I previously launched with Minister Swann but timing will be contingent on resources. The wider use of mediation more generally will require substantial investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what stakeholders are being engaged with as part of the Disabled Facilities Grant review.
answer_text: The Review of (NIHE administered) Private Sector Grants including the Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) takes an interdepartmental approach, being led by the Department for Communities in conjunction with the Department of Health and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  		The Project Board is made up of members from the three organisations alongside a representative from Disability Action, a representative from the Health Trusts, and steps are currently ongoing to secure a service user representative to join the Project Board at the earliest opportunity. 		The Patient and Client Council have recently launched an online survey to seek feedback from individuals who have previous or current experience with the DFG process. This consultation has been shared on the DfC website and social media accounts with the results to be shared with the Project Board to help inform changes that may be made to the current process.  		Any substantial legislative change deemed necessary following the grant review will be consulted on as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the Housing Executive social housing transfer list for (i) two bed accommodation; (ii) three bed accommodation; and (iii) ground floor accommodation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: A breakdown of the social housing transfer list based on the criteria requested is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to address waiting times for urgent orthopaedic surgery in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Addressing waiting times, including for urgent orthopaedics, remains a key priority for me. In April 2022 my Department commissioned an independent review of the elective orthopaedic service in NI to support service rebuild following the pandemic.  Since then, good progress has been made across the region, with some Trusts now exceeding pre-Covid levels of activity. There have been some recent challenges experienced in the Western Trust. The Trust has provided a clear plan on how to recover elective orthopaedics to pre-COVID levels, and I expect this to be achieved by end of March. This will support increased service delivery. In 2022/23, the Western Trust partnered with the Independent Sector to treat patients on orthopaedic waiting lists at the South West Acute Hospital. This was stopped due to financial pressures; however, I hope to restart this initiative, subject to availability of funding.  Transformation of HSC services alone is not enough to remove unacceptably long waiting times.  We need to invest in additionality to reduce waits. Any future funding allocation to help reduce waiting lists in orthopaedics across all Trusts, including the Western Trust, will need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.
